<RELATIONS>I live with my Mummy, Auntie and my 13 year old brother and sister who are twins.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Today we are living in lockdown due to the Covid-19 pandemic.</COVIDNEWS> Even though my <SCHOOL>Mummy and Auntie are keyworkers as they are a Midwife and a teacher I have not had</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>to go to school as they take it in turns to work from home and look after us.</EMPLOYMENT> Today I <ACTIVITIES>woke up at 7.25am and read my book.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>I then had multi grain hoops for breakfast and orange juice.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>Once I was dressed we did PE with Joe Wicks which is very tough but I enjoy it and do it every day.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>Then it was time for the school quiz that the science teacher</SCHOOL> does every week. We scored 13 out of 20. <SCHOOL>I then started my home learning with my PE</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>theory task where I named the bones of the body and named 5 joints and 5 muscles.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>After that my Mummy gave me sentences to write out so I could practice my capital</ACTIVITIES> letters as I keep forgetting to use them! <FOOD>I had a chicken and cheese pitta for lunch and a slice of my brother and sister’s birthday cake which is yummy!</FOOD> <SCHOOL>My history teacher did a</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>virtual lesson which told me about the Mass Observations Archives and that today is</SCHOOL> the day to do it. <ROUTINE>I wanted to do it so people will never forget what the World has been</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>through during Covid-19.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Covid-19 is a very serious virus which needs all our medical knowledge.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>It is scary and lockdown is frustrating because I cannot see my Nanny</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>unless I video call her, I also can’t go out or go to school.</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>I am looking forward to being</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>able to go to the beach once it is safe.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Right now I am off to go for a walk with my</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Mummy, brother and sister and then I have free time where I can chill out!</RELATIONS>

<COVIDNEWS>Let me admit straightaway (as this is anonymous) that I am enjoying lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>I am an 85-year old man living with a charming lady partner (whom I love dearly) in a development beside the Thames.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My wife died 19 years ago leaving me with four</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>children and seven grandchildren.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>The children are most supportive, Skyping with enthusiasm, and my elder daughter sends splendid packages of books and dvd’s.</RELATIONS> What more could one want? <ROUTINE>I am rated as “Extremely clinically vulnerable” (having Churg Strauss, a chronic</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>chest condition) so have been ordered by the Govt.</ROUTINE> not to go outside the boundary of these premises. Luckily these premises include a splendid two or three acre <ACTIVITIES>garden.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Lazy start to the day.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I begin by sending a few fragments of memoire to my family. Apparently they like the War Years best.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>Breakfast is porridge (my partner is Scots)</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>and we then read for a bit.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I’m reading Jorrocks (“Handley Cross”) again, by Surtees.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I have around 300 books on the shelves here and the library also deliver</ROUTINE> interesting packages. <EMOTIONS>We do not have television because we get bored by it.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Mind you, my own pre-occupation is even more tedious - crosswords.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>First one is always the Guardian Crossword at around 6.00 am on the net.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>Then email to see how the family are.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>All well.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Most are still in work, from</EMPLOYMENT> home. <EMPLOYMENT>Two grandsons are working flat out with (telephone) research which is in</EMPLOYMENT> great demand. <ACTIVITIES>Out for forty minute walk round the garden.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>Gardeners hard at work (thank</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>goodness) because yesterday the Thames came up over half of the garden, leaving</ACTIVITIES> much debris. <RELATIONS>Met two equally elderly neighbours, sat down in the sun (a very fine day) to chat about the past, carefully keeping 6ft apart.</RELATIONS> We are surprised that so <ACTIVITIES>few of the residents use the garden.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Just a mother with two toddlers, both of whom</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>are distinctly doubtful about meeting Polly, the resident cat.</EMPLOYMENT> <FOOD>Seems its my turn to cook lunch - Cottage Pie, for which I had downloaded some recipes from the net but not enough to see how to make it. Just breeze into the kitchen, I thought, and assemble a few things under the admiring gaze of my partner. It didn’t work out that way. On entering the kitchen I was mesmerised by the vast selection of pots, pans and ingredients laid out for me. Didn’t know where to start. Partner waited expectantly, got bored and took charge. I did the watching as everything flew into position and a Cottage Pie emerged.</FOOD> The last ten years? <RELATIONS>When my wife died I took a long trip to India.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Returned to UK to meet up again with the gorgeous lady I had met on one of my committees, eventually persuading her to let me move in with her.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>The last ten years have seen me whirled around the world by my travel-loving companion.</RELATIONS> We found South <ROUTINE>America (Argentina, Chile, Ecuador, Peru, etc.) particularly appealing, although my</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Spanish is poor and my Tango is appalling.</ROUTINE> The next ten years - if I am so lucky? More of the same please (but not perhaps <COVIDNEWS>lockdown) and may my children and grandchildren continue to flourish.</COVIDNEWS>

<SCHOOL>How did you hear about the diary day: We were invited by our teacher to partake in the diary entry.</SCHOOL> I am 10 years old and I turn 11 in June. <SCHOOL>I attend prep at a local grammar school.</SCHOOL> I live in a tiny village surrounded by farms, fields and wooded areas. <RELATIONS>I am one of two, I have a brother aged 8 and a 10-month-old puppy, we all live with my mum and dad who I adore.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>This morning was a little different for me as my mum came into wake me, I was confused at first but I looked at my bedroom door and saw a big bag of presents that I had wrapped for my dad the night before.</RELATIONS> Today May 12th is his birthday. <ROUTINE>I jumped out of bed and ran downstairs to give him a big hug.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>I write this diary from my kitchen, we are on lock down and I am required to home school.</SCHOOL> <DIGITAL>I use my mum's laptop computer and iPad so she has to squeeze in her work when she can.</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>On writing this, I realise looking back that the lock down, how at times it has been very frustrating and confusing, but I am reminded this morning just how much my family and I have adapted.</EMOTIONS> <SCHOOL>I keep thinking about the government's announcement about kids returning to school.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>My mum has just told me that it is looking very likely that I will be going back to school possibly as early as June 1st.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>I can't wait as I am longing to be reunited with my friends and teachers.</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>Oh, and I love the school dinners.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>My mum and dad however have reservations, they are unsure today how they feel about me going back.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>My dog has been licking my arm on and off all morning, she wants a treat and a hug from me, she adores being heavily petted and loved.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>It's a nice interruption from home schooling which can get very tense as we are expected to hand in assignments, I try as best as I can to stick to my timetable as I am constantly worried about falling behind.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>My shelves are really messy, my books and pens are everywhere and my mum keep coming in to see me to check I am OK but she always gives me a look to remind me I have to tidy up, It has become our little joke.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I'm looking out the kitchen window.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>It isn't as sunny today as It has been which in some ways is helpful as I don’t want to go in the garden as much as I have wanted to when it is really hot.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Although right now I would love to curl up on my sofa and watch a movie with my brother.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>He keeps going upstairs to play with his toys and my mum is trying to get him to do his spellings.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>It's hilarious (not for my mum though).</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>I try and Facetime my god sister everyday as she is an only child and its my way of relaxing after a day of studying.</DIGITAL> Oh! <FOOD>I've just remembered I am going to eat lots and lots of cake.</FOOD> <EMOTIONS>Today is a great day.</EMOTIONS>

text: <ROUTINE>Manchester This morning I woke up at 7am, I made my bed and went down stairs.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Today is a bit colder than it has been recently.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>I had some Shreddies for my breakfast with my little brother and we watched Ben and Holly whilst we ate (his favourite programme).</FOOD> <ROUTINE>After that I went to get dressed, I put on a jumper and some leggings.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>A few minutes later I did some work, first I did a math lesson and then I did English.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>English was about trolls and how not all of them are bad.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>It’s so boring in lockdown we have nothing to do.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>We can’t meet up with friends and we can’t go to school to see our friends.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>We’ve been in lockdown for over 6 weeks now.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Hopefully life will be back to normal again soon and they will find a vaccine for the coronavirus so we can save people in the future.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>Anyway, I’m going to have lunch now and do some reading and timestables practice this afternoon.</ACTIVITIES>

<COVIDNEWS>We are in a global pandemic, called the coronavirus. The whole world has been affected.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>I think that Britain is the worst affected place, and has the highest number of deaths - today, the count stands at 40,000 deaths in the UK.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>We have been in national lockdown for 7 weeks (since schools shut).</COVIDNEWS> <SCHOOL>They are talking about letting reception and years 1 and 6 go back to school soon, but not my year yet.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>It has been very hard not being able to see some of my closest friends.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>Instead, I have been keeping in touch with them via email and hangouts (on Gmail), and been doing regular ZOOM calls with each other to talk.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>I have also been playing multiplayer games on my X-Box one S, like Minecraft and Roblox.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>I have been doing home-schooling for 5 weeks (with a 2-week Easter break).</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>My class teacher has been uploading us Maths, English, Geography and History tasks through an app called Seesaw.</SCHOOL> <DIGITAL>I have been doing them using: Word, Power point and google docs.</DIGITAL> <SCHOOL>My parents have been (along with most other parents in the UK) teaching me from home.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>Today, I have done my Maths and have been reading Harry Potter (for the 4th time).</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>This diary entry counts as half of my English for today, although I do not look on it as schoolwork or a chore.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>Later I will do a bit more English, cooking (cookies), and spelling.</SCHOOL> <ACTIVITIES>Once school is over, I will (hopefully) play ‘Sea of Thieves’ (my favourite video game), read more Harry Potter, listen to Capital UK (a radio station), and play with my beautiful cat, Pebbles (a Bengal cat).</ACTIVITIES>

<DIGITAL>This morning I woke up at about 8, and before I got ready to do some home schooling work, I went on my Xbox for about an hour.</DIGITAL> <SCHOOL>After that I went downstairs and done a maths lesson.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>After that I had some breakfast.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This morning I had cereal.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>After that I done a German lesson, and now I am doing an English lesson which is to write this diary entry.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>After this I might start another piece of English work and finish it after I have my lunch.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>When I have finished all of my work, I may go on a walk and possibly play on my Xbox with some friends, and then have some dinner.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>This evening I will just relax and possibly Facetime my cousins and then go to sleep tonight and wake up the next morning and do all this again!</DIGITAL>

Who knows, who cares? <ROUTINE>This is my entry for the Mass Observation May 12th one day diary project, written on Tuesday</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>12th May 2020 at approximately 11am.</ROUTINE> I am a 30-year-old white male, born and raised in North London, currently residing in Arnos Grove, North London. <EMPLOYMENT>I am currently unemployed as I am</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>training to be a secondary school English teacher with a view to completing my course and</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>starting in a school this coming September.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>At the time of writing this, the world is in the throes of the Covid-19 pandemic; there is a global</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>wide lockdown in place and the vast majority of people are under quarantine, self-isolation,</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>house arrest, whatever you want to call it.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Only ‘key workers’ i.e.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>people working in hospitals</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>or in manufacturing are going into work but basically all businesses (including schools) are</EMPLOYMENT> closed until further notice. <ROUTINE>We are permitted to leave the house for two reasons: 1) to go out</ROUTINE> to buy food. <ACTIVITIES>2) to go out for some fresh air/exercise (I went for a run this morning with my girlfriend who I live with, along with our housemate and cat) and we must make sure that we</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>practice good ‘social distancing’ measures i.e.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>avoid coming into close contact with other people who are not in our household at the risk of exacerbating the spread of the virus.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Every day we are briefed by the current Prime Minister, Boris Johnson, and his team of medical advisory bigwigs on the news, where he espouses pretty useless and contradictory diatribes about British resilience and staying safe by avoiding going out in public, unless you absolutely</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>have to, or if the weather’s really nice and you just can’t resist playing frisbee in the park with your dog.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>At this point in the day, I’m usually playing guitar or listening to music upstairs.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>It’s</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>boring to hear the same old rhetoric day in and day out so I choose to ignore it for the most</ROUTINE> part. <COVIDNEWS>Many people are finding it hard being kept indoors for such a long period of time (lockdown</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>officially started around the week beginning Monday 16th March) so it’s been almost 2 months</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>of living like this and I, for one, I’m quite content with the situation at present.</EMOTIONS> I am not one of <ROUTINE>these mongoloids who is desperate for human interaction and validation or who craves for a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>return to the ‘normal’ way of life, far from it.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I don’t drink so I couldn’t care less that</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Wetherspoons is probably going to go into administration (good, as far as I’m concerned) I am</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>enjoying my ‘self-isolation’ by playing guitar, writing new music, improving old music and enhancing my skills as a musician.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I like to read where possible and I am still in the process</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>of completing my teacher training course (although I am procrastinating by doing this instead of my assignment that’s due in a couple of weeks).</SCHOOL> <EMOTIONS>I should stress that this so called ‘isolation’</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>is quite normal for me anyway; I’m somewhat of a recluse and like to stay indoors and work</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>on productive artistic activities or self-improvement (I exercise at home a lot).</ACTIVITIES> Once the <COVIDNEWS>lockdown is eventually lifted, I’ll more than likely still be staying at home, in my element, with</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>the guitar on my lap and some Black or Death Metal playing through my headphones.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>At the risk of sounding like a cold, heartless piece of shit, I honestly couldn’t be happier with this gift of time that has been given to me to pursue artistic and self-improvement endeavours.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Obviously it sucks that people are dying (the death toll in the UK is around 33,000) but as Eric</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>Idle says “Always look on the bright side of life!” I very much doubt that anyone reading this</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>won’t take this diatribe with a pinch of salt, but if you do and are deeply offended or saddened,</ROUTINE> tough. <ROUTINE>You won’t get any sympathy from me if you choose to be a victim.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Things aren’t all roses and sunshine for me though.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My family live really close by, but I can’t</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>just go and see them whenever I want.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>I still have to complete assignments based on now speculative teaching practices (basically shooting in the dark) although my tutor tells me I’m doing quite well since I have a pretty good command of the English language (hooray for thesaurus’s).</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>But do you hear me complaining?</ROUTINE> No. <ROUTINE>Sure, things could be a hell of a lot worse</ROUTINE> for me; I am young, fit, in good health, no pre-existing medical conditions and I live in a nice, <ROUTINE>fairly secluded part of London.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I also have an artistic and creative mind so I am able to use my</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>imagination and work ethic to stem any potential boredom.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Basically, I’ve got better things to</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>do than complain on social media or die from coronavirus.</COVIDNEWS> Again, I know it sounds harsh for <ACTIVITIES>someone reading this (if anyone even is, who cares!) but I’m using this project as an</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>opportunity to vent my inner most thoughts and feelings about the current state of affairs in</ROUTINE> the world in 2020. <EMOTIONS>I get frustrated when people say that you should pursue ‘happiness’ as</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>opposed to pursuing ‘contentment’.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Happiness is fleeting; one minute you’re happy, the next</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>you’re dead from coronavirus.</COVIDNEWS> Go figure. <ROUTINE>I prefer to make positive change so that when the</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>storm does come, I’m ready with a somewhat positive (though someone else reading this</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>might disagree) outlook.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Maybe positive is stretching it; realistic is probably more accurate.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Much of the country is using this time to improve their mental health and wellbeing and more</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>power to them as far as I’m concerned.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But we each have our own unique take on certain</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>situations and respond to them as such, is all I’m trying to unintelligibly get out of this.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>For some reason, everyone thought that a new decade of this century would be somehow so</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>much better than the previous one.</ROUTINE> Guess what? <ROUTINE>Turns out you can’t predict the future!</ROUTINE> For <ROUTINE>me, however, I’m cautiously optimistic about things in general.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’m certainly in a much better</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>place than I was even a year ago, never mind 10 years ago.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Anyway, that’s probably enough</ROUTINE> from me. <ACTIVITIES>If anyone has read this, hopefully you’ve gotten an ‘alternative’ insight into a</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>somewhat chaotic realm of existence; maybe you’re like me and you refuse to play ball and</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>accept that things are really shit when really this could be used as a time for self-reflection and</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>improvement, maybe you’re reading this in the future and there’s some other type of</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>global/local catastrophe going on around you and you’re not really that phased because you’re</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>creative and have time and strength on your side.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Maybe the people in charge of Mass Obs</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Archive won’t accept my entry for their records because I’ve used swear words.</ROUTINE> Again, who cares? <ROUTINE>Pondering and pontification are not part of my MO.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’m just making the most of this</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>situation by using this as an outlet for my inner most thoughts and feelings.</ROUTINE> As far as the future is concerned: Who knows? <ROUTINE>The sun is shining as I write this entry and I intend to make hay</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>while it still hangs in the sky.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Although I won’t be doing it outside, fuck that.</ROUTINE>

I live in Trimley St Mary, with my mother, step-father, and baby brother. I go to the primary school where I am in Year 2. Since Monday 23 March, I have been home learning with work set by my teacher. Today, I watched the movie Madagascar (“I like to move it move it, you like to - move it!”). For exercise, I went for a bike ride and talked to my Grandma from her door. I stayed 2 metres away at the end of her front path. I also did Joe Wicks P.E. session. I did some baking. I made some scones, but they were a bit flat! I made some fairy cakes, and some jam tarts - YUM! I did some colouring and some painting. I fixed my ruler. "

Written by a retired Senior Medical Secretary living in Rushmere St. Andrew, near Ipswich. Tuesday 12th May and still in isolation. It was on the 17th March, St. Patrick's Day that I first started to write a journal and here we are eight weeks later still staying at home with not much hope of the lock down being lifted for my age group. At least we can walk and this is what I try to do every day. If the weather is inclement I pedal away on my exercise bike. Andre Rieu? Most days stretch out before me broken by telephone calls from friends and family, always trying to motivate myself to do something worthwhile. But not today as Tuesday is my busy day. So up early to a sunny start, a quick breakfast, making sure I am eating the right foods. At just before 11am I get my iPad and log into Zoom and join up with my local exercise class. This is the third week and we all seem to have conquered the intricacies of this modern technology turning the "mute" button off when instructed. This took a bit of organising! It is lovely to see friends from our weekly exercise class on screen. Time then for a visit to the garden and a bit of weeding, having a large garden I am grateful to have a gardener who still comes keeping his distance as directed by the Government. Time for lunch and then just before 3pm I link up by phone with my Acupuncturist who leads a meditation with her patients. This has a very calming effect on me and after that it is time to watch the daily Government bulletin on the television. I am just getting over watching the 75 year celebration of the ending of World War Two on the 8th May. For me it is an emotional time, like for many others. As a small girl of five on a sunny afternoon I was playing ball with a young airman of eighteen. Next day I went into the hospital to have my tonsils out. When I came home I was told that my friendly young airman had been killed. I come from a military family but this young man was the only member of the family who did not return after the war. Hence each anniversary I still mourn the loss of this young life. Looking back over the last ten years they were laced with happy and sad things. For us all as anno domini marches on we lose our friends and loved ones. For me it was my husband and the most traumatic was having to watch my younger sister suffer with Parkinson's dementia, passing away just before the lock down. Highlights were many with marriages and new lives coming into the family. May 8th was a special day for me when I was enrolled as a Girl Guide, the first in a newly formed company. Two years ago we celebrated 70 years and I organised a church service and luncheon in my home. It is quite amazing that twelve of the very early Guides still meet once a month. Of course this has now been put on hold until the lock down has been removed for we oldies. Today being the 12th May is a very special date. It is International Nurses Day and also the birthday of Florence Nightingale. The whole country is now realising how much we rely on the NHS, from the porters, cleaners and everyone up to the nurses and Consultants. What brave people they are keeping the hospitals open at this time of crisis. I have to be grateful to them as my own grand daughter's partner, a junior doctor on the front line, caught the virus and she also caught it. Luckily they are now well again. Despite the terrible pandemic which has taken so many lives worldwide communities have come together and as the Queen said "We will meet again". I look forward to the time when I can join in with the WI activities and holidays, long walks with the group I belong to and of course having my family around me. I am left wondering when my son and his family in Sweden will be able to visit, not this June as planned, and a grandson’s wedding has been postponed until 2021. However we must look forward and for me my holiday to Harrogate postponed on 13th April 2020 has now been booked for 26th July 2021. Hopefully by that time a vaccine will be available and life will have returned to some normality! "

through a window.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I worked through an online NHS exercise in controlling Anxiety ahead of my telephone appointment with a local mental health service tomorrow.</COVIDNEWS> nesting box (coal tits are using it) by banging on the bedroom window and watched the futile) courtship dance of a wood pigeon.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Defrosted lasagne for tea (still plenty of frozen cooked meals).</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Husband's homemade flapjack is great comfort food, especially when warmed up and scooped with a spoon.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I can't bring myself to watch news bulletins any more so learned from the BBC website about a gun massacre at a maternity hospital in Kabul.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It's hard to put prayers into words sometimes, so I just mentally reach towards God.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Hoping for better times.</EMOTIONS>

<ROUTINE>Dear Diary, Today has been quite a fun day.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Although, I awoke this morning terrified in bed after a horrible nightmare but at least I don’t remember anymore.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>When I got up first thing this morning everyone was asleep and only I lay awake, so I read a book called Harry Potter and the Order of the phoenix.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>A little while later, I fell asleep again and woke up at the same time as my mum.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>We both went down stairs and got to work.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>My mum went to start her lesson online and I still had an hour and a half before my online lesson so I printed off my work.</SCHOOL> <ACTIVITIES>Then I decided to do some colourful doodles.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>During the next part of the day I joined my classes online.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>My first three lessons were English, Spanish and maths.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>English was quite fun because we were answering questions about an exciting but difficult book that I’m reading.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>At the end of the lesson I stayed behind to finish some questions on the text.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>The next lesson I had was Spanish and I did some work sheets about directions and weather.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>Then I had maths which was ok but I didn’t really like it that much because I find graphs a bit tricky.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>Afterwards I had lunch and accidently skipped PE which now I will have to do tomorrow instead.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>In the evening, my family and I had dinner and dessert.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>Today we had meet pie, potatoes and beans, it was delicious! It was followed by homemade pancakes with Canadian maple syrup or lemon and sugar. It was mouth-watering and my taste buds were in heaven. One of my favourite puddings ever!</FOOD> <ROUTINE>Now it is the last part of my day and I am writing this, which has taken over twenty minutes to do.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>As I write the final words I’m getting ready to read my book and go to bed.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Then tomorrow I will wake up refreshed and ready for tomorrow.</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>The Thames is quiet at 6.30 this morning.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>And it's beautiful.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Clear blue sky.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Cold.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I'm married, male, 65, renting an apartment with my wife in Rotherhithe, South East London.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Permanent home is Galloway, Scotland.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>A contract for my wife has brought us to London for a spell.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>We've been here 18 months now.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>My occupation: teacher, framemaker, painter.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>I'm self-employed.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>A friend describes my life as a form of 'cultural crofting'.</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>During the coronavirus crisis my life as a teacher (art history) and framemaking has come to a halt.</SCHOOL> <ACTIVITIES>I can paint and prepare art history talks meanwhile.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Today.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Painting in the morning.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>Working on a series of abstract paintings.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>They are completely new to me and strange and exciting.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>I don't want to break the spell.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Some work, some don't.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>It's odd to feel like this because at the same time you can't but help also feel deeply anxious that so many people are facing such an uncertain future.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>As we and our children are too.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Who is going to be looking at paintings?</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>My wife is able to work from home.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>A bonus for me as we can have a midday meal together.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>During the crisis everyone has been encouraged by the government to get out and take some exercise. Until yesterday, once a day. Now, more than once if you wish.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>I go for a bicycle ride after lunch. To Surrey Quays and back through Suffolk Park.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>There is more traffic on the roads now.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I notice that a carwash business is open.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>People are beginning to restart their businesses if they can.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>In the afternoon a mix of admin in the form of starting the annual process of gathering all the information and documents I need to put together for my self assessment form for the Inland Revenue.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Break off from this to continue painting until 5.45.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Then make supper while my wife goes for a walk.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>In the evening, having supper, we watch a BBC2 documentary on Miles Davis.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>This is on bbciplayer.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>We pause this at 8.00pm to have a Zoom catch up with our twin sons, who are 27.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>They both live (separately - one sharing a house with friends, one living with his Japanese girlfriend) in south London and both are able to work from home.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>They are both well and although like everyone, dealing with an unclear future, they are both cheerful.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>We talk about the crisis, family news and plans for the coming weekend. How many people we are allowed to meet. One of us one other person not of our household it seems.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>We have been told by the government to use our common sense.</COVIDNEWS> <SCHOOL>At times it feels as though we are all back at school.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>Break the rules and there are penalties if caught.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>What will the future hold?</ROUTINE>

<EMPLOYMENT>Male, aged 60, retired consultant physician, living in South London.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>Married with one son aged 26</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>and one daughter aged 21.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Three of us (myself, wife and daughter here at home; son living away).</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Now in what is our 7th week of “lock-down”, which was imposed March 23/24 2020, after a brief</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>period of increasing demands to change behaviour.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Just at the point where the most severe and</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>restricting component of the lockdown is beginning to be released, just a little.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Most days are the same – up to now, we have been strongly disencouraged to go out of our house</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>more than once daily – for exercise, essential trips e.g.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>doctor, to pick up prescriptions, or help</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>people.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Food is delivered as per normal for us, but trips to shops are few, and not comfortable as</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>many shops cannot enforce proper social distancing.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>But it is acceptable.</ROUTINE>  <EMPLOYMENT>Quiet day – some telephone conferences etc which is part of my work now.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Around 50% of people</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>will use some VOIP or other way to communicate – the sense of isolation is vastly reduced by being</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>able to ring, text, tweet, email, videocall, and otherwise contact people.</DIGITAL> <FOOD>During this lock-down I am learning how to cook. It could be argued that I have taken my time over this, being 60, and with a shape which strongly suggests that food and I have been good friends for some time. But, I decided that I should. Tonight’s dish was Beef Bourgignon, done rather well, to a Mary Berry recipe. She does like her tipple – a lot of red wine, and brandy, but everyone agreed it was a success. In fact, all of the dishes I have prepared over the last 5-6 weeks (one evening meal / week) have been successful, and certainly stretched my very modest, quite threadbare, culinary skills.</FOOD> <EMOTIONS>It is not an easy period – a lot of personal and professional uncertainties.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I take relief in reading</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>(sadly, often books about pandemics), listening to music (Mahler, and Brahms, today), Tweeting, and doing some blog posts (again, quite a new thing for me).</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>I only hope that his pandemic recedes, that my family can be spared personal or professional</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>adverse consequences, and that the country can get back on its feet again.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I am frankly sceptical</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>about that last point.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I hope you enjoyed a tiny slice of 12th May 2020</EMOTIONS>

<EMPLOYMENT>I am a 37 year old Irish academic.I am a woman living in ings Heath in South</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Birmingham and I work at the University of .</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>I live with my partner, our dog</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>and cat.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My partner is also an academic.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>She is American.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>She and I have been</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>together almost 5 years.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>This is the 7th (I think?) we k of lockdown. The "stay safe" novelty phase has worn off. Mostly we have be n fairly obedient.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Today the government relaxed the rules</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>slightly to permit people to go for socially distanced walks with friends.</RELATIONS> But we have <ROUTINE>done that several times already.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Today I met a friend from work who lives alone, for</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>our third such walk.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>On another friend's birthday we walked in Uffmoor Wood - we</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>were almost the only people there.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I think as lawyers - we are academic lawyers - we have be n more sceptical about the rules, while appreciating the need for social distancing.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>My partner has been delivering food for the Solidarity Kitchen 3 times a week.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Several people on our road do it, and I have gone with her twice so far.</ROUTINE> The food is <ROUTINE>vegan and cooked from donations and supermarket surpluses.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The people receiving</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>the deliveries are visibly poor and often elderly or chronically ill.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>It feels empowering to do something to help.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>We went sorting potatoes for Slow Food Birmingham- rescuing" spuds that had been rejected by McCains
column7: and getting them ready to be prepped for the Kitchen. That felt really useful.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>We aren't wearing masks yet and are probably more lax about hand hygiene than we</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>should be - even if we are more careful than normal.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We probably think this is offset</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>by avoiding supermarkets and shops
column8: by our relative youth and by keeping our</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>distance as much as possible when walking the dog in the local park.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>The park is</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>much more full now - as the gyms and other places have shut down.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We get a lot of deliveries from local businesses - beer especially.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We collect bread</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>from Loaf in Stirchley and meat from Rossiters.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Neighbourfood delivers vegetables.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We have always eaten well - supporting local producers means a lot to us and we</ROUTINE> want to se them survive this. <ROUTINE>I can't say I am engaged in much self improvement.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I did a lot of tidying and reading</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>in the beginning
column9: but haven't taken up running or yoga.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My partner has always done</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>yoga and now every day practice has made her visibly stronger.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>We are working from home
column10: which means a lot of Zoom meetings and an incredible</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>amount of stress.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>There is a sense that universities - wealthy ones like our employers - will use this as an opportunity to justify cuts and layoffs. ayoffs of precarious workers se m not even to be acknowledged as such.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>My partner is a union organiser</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>at and their management attitude is brutal - antagonistic toward the union and reluctant to recognise that th y are deliberately cutting from the bottom as workload increases dramatically. We had been on strike in the days leading up to</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>lockdown - that fe ls like a different world now.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>As to the government - it feels utt rly hopeless
column11: and as though resistance is</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>impossible.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>We are safe becaus we are middle class.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>Everyone else is out.</ROUTINE> We can <EMOTIONS>only hope that unions can protect their workers.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Things are probably better in</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Ireland - despite the lack of a proper government.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>It feels awful not to know when</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>will next be back.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>That's all now.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Suitably dull
column12: I hope.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Thank you for your work.</EMPLOYMENT>"

text: <ROUTINE>Woke in the morning and looked out the window at the blossom on the apple trees and the lush</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>green trees of many gardens of Moseley beyond.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This is an important part of my day in which I take</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>a moment to appreciate the amount of greenery in the UK’s second city, as well as the amount of</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>wildlife, as it’s ordinarily evidenced by fresh holes in the lawn, dug by the local foxes and badgers.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Today I can’t see any.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I was awoken by my daughter making her morning murmurings over the monitor.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>She greeted me with her happy grin and gave me her dummy.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Usually she shouts ‘agua’ first, at 20 months old she</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>seems to know more Spanish than English, today though it was ‘up’, keen to get out of bed and start</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>the day.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My wife walked in the room and helped change the nappy and apply ointment,</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>unfortunately my daughter has dry skin and suffers from eczema, this is kept at bay with ointment</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>and non-bio washing detergent, and alleviated with silk leggings.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>As usual I have my cereal breakfast, with an array of turmeric, chia seeds and Maca powder (this 'be healthy' trend sweeps up everyone in some way), my daughter has her weetabix and berries, and my wife her chocolate weetabix. For drinks French press coffee, water and tea respectively.</FOOD> <DIGITAL>My wife continued her online teaching course (she recently left teaching owing to the stress</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>involved, government austerity has led to very poor conditions for teaching), I looked after my</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>daughter until lunch, then she went down for her nap, and I went into the garage to do DIY.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>For lunch me and my Wife had fish finger sandwiches, a timeless classic upgraded to modern standard with the addition of brioche buns.</FOOD> <COVIDNEWS>It was a lovely sunny day if not a bit cold, we did our lockdown</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>exercise by walking up the hill to Swanshurst Park.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>We have cut our takeaways down to one a month, and as always had curry. Birmingham is still the best place to buy curry in the UK, however I have noticed over the last four or five years that other areas of the country generally confined to suburban areas have attained a close standard of quality for their Indian sub-continent food. However, I must add that when I had a curry in Brick Lane in 2013 and in the same year a curry in Manchester’s curry mile, both were very poor.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>No cider tonight, as we shall try for another child in the coming months.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The temptation is great</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>though, I have over 100 remaining, blends of dabinett, foxwhelp and harry masters jersey, with</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>some perry.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I buy the bulk of the apples from an orchard owner in Canon Pyon, and I am given some</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>pears from a nice couple who own an old orchard near Orleton.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>The trees are old and I routinely take graftwood to try and propagate new trees.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I enjoy my regular trips to Herefordshire,</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I am furloughed. It’s a good time to wind down, but sadly it’s at a great cost.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>I feel, for the benefit of</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>anyone reading this in the future, the need to explain how badly I feel this country is currently being</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>run.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>The angry anti-immigration sentiment which won the Brexit referendum for the leavers</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>continues to ferment as well as prop up this poor, substandard government which is running public</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>sector services into the ground, including the NHS. The country is becoming morally, socially and monetarily bankrupt.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>I wish my daughter could grow up in happier times, that said I grew up as one</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>of Thatcher’s children but still enjoyed the fairer society New Labour brought.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>I hope times change</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>soon, if not we may be forced to move abroad, away from family and friends.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>France seems like an</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>attractive prospect, but how long until they succumb to this hatred and fear.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>In many ways I am</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>surprised it has happened on this side of Le Manche first.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Which leaves me torn between staying with family, friends, familiarity, and cider, or leaving for a</RELATIONS> better but very different life somewhere else. <ROUTINE>Not a simple choice, but these are not simple times.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Peace.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Male, 37 years old, Moseley, Birmingham, married, father, surveyor.</RELATIONS>

<ROUTINE>I wake up, look behind the curtains sitting in my bed.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>It's a nice day in Belfast, I check my phone and see a message on social media from an old mate I haven't spoken to in ages.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I get up and dressed.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I go downstairs and have breakfast watching a few episodes of Bugs Bunny from my Looney Tunes boxset and then go out to the garden with a coffee for a smoke.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I then go through my routine of brushing my teeth and using the bathroom before replying to the message I got.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I hear the bin lorry coming up the cul-de-sac I live in and I look out my window and there's also a delivery van and a construction van arrived.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I soon go out for a walk with my Dad round the streets where we live in south Belfast.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>We chat about what we walk past including big detached houses with luscious gardens and a graffiti sticker of which I know the local artist.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>Back at the house and I have a bowl of soup with tea and toast in the kitchen with my parents before going up to my room for a phone appointment at 1:00 with my ASD psychotherapist.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>I have a fairly long chat in depth about my life and mental health at the moment.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>After this I go outside for a smoke and sit on the bench in the front garden, I enjoy the sunshine.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I then go back inside to my room and continue listening to a playlist I've added a lot more songs to called 'Melodies For Maladies'.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>While I do, I work on a drawing I'm doing using red colouring pencil and finish this layer of it.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I then go outside to the back garden for another 'cuppa and a smoke while my Dad cuts the grass in the front garden.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I sit on a chair after and listen to the playlist.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>My cat's outside with me on her lead.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The lead after a while gets tangled as she goes under the fence.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I didn't notice at first because of my music.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>The other young men next door are walking about the garden and lifting weights in the garage on the other side of the fence.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>The thud of the weight being put on the ground made me look round.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I gently encourage her back from under it and bring her inside for her dinner.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Later in the day, my Mum's back from visiting my elderly Granda who lives a couple of miles away.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I greet her from the front garden where I moved to as she comes up the front steps.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>She then goes in and cooks a couple of small burgers for me, and herself, my Dad and I use up leftovers that are in the fridge.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>We watch the next episode of a family drama series that we've started the previous couple of nights.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I then bring out the plates and load the dishwasher and get myself a drink and a rolled cigarette and go out to the garden with my cat.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My Dad then comes out to tell me to come inside as it's nearly 8:30, time for shining a light through our window for the nurses.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I gently stub out my cigarette which I only started and leave my drink and the cat outside.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We all go upstairs to the top of the house.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My Dad and I use a desk lamp I brought and my Mum in the next door room shines a light through the window and starts playing her drum kit loudly.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>When the minute on the clock has passed and we seemed to be the only ones doing it, fun as it was, I go back out and have my smoke and drink and bring the cat in.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>That about brings me up to where I am now writing this on my laptop in my room lit with colourful LED lights and the curtains closed after admiring the pink sunset.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>I listened to the playlist again for a bit, Deep Purple A Child In Time comes on.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>How appropriate I thought.</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>Force base 4 miles from town.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I am a firefighter, with the rank of Station Captain, and am in charge of a crew of 12 other firefighters on a 24hrs on, 48hrs off shift system.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>This has continued pretty much unchanged throughout the Coronavirus period.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>I have been married for 30 years and have one son who lives in Manchester.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I have 5 years left to serve before I retire from the job.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>As usual on a duty day, I get up at 0500 and creep around so as not to wake my wife, although she does always wake up, we keep up the pretence.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>A cup of tea, and at 0600 I leave the house with a carry out coffee and take myself to a local BP petrol station to sit for a while, drink my coffee and read the paper, all the while with half a mind on the day ahead.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>Because I have been on leave for a couple of shifts I have no real idea what might face me when I arrive at the fire station, who will be on duty today, who may have gone off sick, what sort of mechanical state the fire engines will be in, what calls the crews will have had over these past days.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>At 0645 I roll up to the main gate of the base, pull up my facemask and show my ID to the Security Police, briefly pulling down my mask to show my face, and am then waved through.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>I am at the station for 0650 and , deep breath, walk in.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>It is now a one-for-one swap instead of a full crew handover. My opposite number give me a brief exchange of information, I look at the clipboard roster, and he gets his bag, puts his fire kit away, and leaves for home at 0710.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>The station is now mine, and at 0730 all my crew are in for a roll call and to check the trucks, equipment, fuel, breathing sets.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>It’s a cool, but sunny day, so after teabreak at 0900, the crew get changed into leggings and boots and tunics and go out to do some training, but I am chained to the computer, catching up on emails, checking spreadsheets, approving leave, signing timecards and doing the 100 and 1 things a supervisor has to do.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Its quiet in the office, and outside I can hear a ventilation fan powering up as they blow fake smoke through the training house.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>As usual, time runs away.</ROUTINE> Before I know it, it is 1200 and time for lunch, then the afternoon continues in a similar vein. <DIGITAL>Computer, tasks for the crew, a kit run to</DIGITAL>

I am a male, 61 years old, and in good health. I live in Hailsham in East Sussex. <RELATIONS>I am married, have 3 adult step-children and 4 grandchildren between the ages of 2 and 8.</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>I am a retired teacher.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>My wife and I live in a large 4 bedroomed house.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Normally my step-daughter and 2 of the grandchildren live here also.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My step-daughter though is a nurse, so the children have gone to their Dad's in Ninfield, since he has no work in because of the lockdown, and it's safer for them.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>My step-daughter, after work, comes here and self-isolates for 24 hours then, several showers and changes of clothes later, she goes to them.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>My day began as it always does with a light breakfast in front of the TV {BBC Breakfast}.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I feed the outside fish and then make my list for the day.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This always happens, but more so now.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This always begins with some standard entries: 1.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Make your list 2.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Clear social media then ignore for the day 3.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>Read 4.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Walk 5.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Garden clear x 20 {items} 6.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Drive weeds x 10 7. Thin out the big bamboo x 20 8.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Tidy through the house.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>9.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Scrabble 10. Chess or Backgammon 11.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>Write something creative Today, after breakfast, I spent time printing out worksheets from the grandchildren's school.</SCHOOL> <EMOTIONS>I'm happy to do this but got irritated by the constant coloured backgrounds on power points which kill my ink!</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Then I realised that printing in grayscale stopped the problem.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>They just don't look so nice.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>At 9.30 my wife and I drove down to Eastbourne and parked at the foot of Beachy Head, by Bede's School.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>We met our younger son and his girlfriend and went for a 7000 pace walk over the top and round the bottom.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>We think we were within Government guidelines because I was meeting her, and my wife was meeting him .</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>but who really knows?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We dropped into Morrison's on the way back for petrol at less than a pound a litre.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>We were going to shop as well but the queue was just too long.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Back at the house we had lunch in the garden whilst reading.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>In my case the book was "Shantaram" by Gregory David Roberts.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>Very interesting if you've travelled in India, although also scary because I was travelling there, although briefly, at the time that the book is set and it's meant to be biographical.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Next it was garden list time. Today's included making a bamboo fence from the thinned poles mentioned above and some string. We do 10 poles a day {they are quite short, about 80cms} and today was the last day.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>After that it was the usual tidying.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Tenable" the quiz came next. We record multiple episodes.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Then I entered the BBC One Show Chelsea Garden Competition {last week we got included in the Museum of the Home display
column7: so we might be on a role}.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>After that I drove up to town to post a handmade card and DVD of the kids to my wife's mum.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>She is 90 soon and we won't be seeing her.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>First missed Birthday in a long time.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Annoyingly the post office was shut so we did some research
column8: put enough stamps on it and posted it elsewhere.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>Later

text: I live in Birmingham and am 46 years old. <EMOTIONS>I’m a single woman and happy to be so.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I work as an</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>academic at a large university.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>During lockdown I’m working from home; I’m fortunate to have a nice</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>house with plenty of space, two cats to keep me company and a small garden.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Today, 12th May 2020, I woke up around 7, but didn’t get out of bed until just after 8.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I fed my cats,</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>exchanged some texts with my parents and sister, and ate breakfast (cereal, fruit juice, and a cup of</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>tea).</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>During the day I had two video calls for work, each an hour long, and I did various other admin</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>and research tasks.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>I went out in the morning to buy a loaf of bread from our local bakery.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>I was</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>feeling quite stressed and emotional about the pandemic today, and took a bit of time out in the</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>morning to do some crochet which I find very relaxing.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>I made a salad for lunch</FOOD> <RELATIONS>and video called my parents who live in another town.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>We did the crossword together and had a chat.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>It feels like a long time since I’ve seen them, and, although they are in good health, I’m concerned about them getting ill as they are both over 70. It’s reassuring to talk to them.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>After a reasonably productive afternoon’s work, I stopped at about 5pm.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>I went for a short walk around the local park (25 minutes), happy to see geese and goslings.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I try to get out for a</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>walk each day, mostly because it keeps my spirits up.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>My evening was quite busy: I spent an hour meeting with an online music group.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>In normal times we</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>meet once a week to play together, and during lockdown it’s lovely to have a practice session together.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>My food delivery arrived from a supermarket.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>For the first month or so of lockdown I was</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>going to the supermarket, but now it seems more online deliveries have been added, so I’m really</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>happy to have my food delivered.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>I had added a friend’s weekly shop to mine, and at 7:15 I drove 5</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>minutes down the road to deliver his shopping.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>We had a brief chat with me standing at the end of</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>his path.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Then I came home, cooked my dinner and ate it, chatting on a video call to my sister.</DIGITAL> I <ACTIVITIES>loaded the dishwasher, tidied the kitchen, and sat down to write this diary entry and read my novel (The Mirror and the Light).</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I went to bed at about 11pm.</ROUTINE>

<EMPLOYMENT>Age 48, widowed, mother, work in public sector, live in rural Devon.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>It was a struggle to get up this morning - after the kids took ages to get to bed last night for various reasons (reorganising bedroom, schoolwork, spilling tea down the side of their mattress!), I had moved the alarm clock from 6.55 to 7.25.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Downstairs Mum was getting cereal out for breakfast.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>She had been planning to make eggy bread for the kids but one of the children had used up all the eggs making hash brown waffles (delicious!) at the weekend (and also me making omelettes the day before).</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>Sometimes we might have nipped round to the shops to get the thing we had run out of - in this case eggs - but we are trying to cut down the amount of times we go shopping at the moment.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I showed Mum a couple of things I'd seen on Facebook while we waited for the kids to come down - one an online video with an estate agent and someone we know who runs a local cider factory.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>It was entertaining and went into detail of how the coronavirus had affected his online business and how he was managing.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Although he acknowledged that things might not change for a while he was optimistic about the future, and said ultimately people wanted to be drinking in social situations.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I also showed Mum a funny video that was making fun of the fact that Boris Johnson had given such an unclear message to people on Sunday.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>'Stay Alert': what does that mean? That people weren't really clear on the changes of lockdown rules.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>After breakfast I sat down with the girls to work while the children did their home schooling.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>One of them has refused to do it downstairs with us from the beginning, and prefers to get on with it in his bedroom.</SCHOOL> <DIGITAL>I worked on emails, Facebook and Twitter posts, and did some planning for some online events I've got coming up.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>Mid-morning I took a break to make coffee and make the children crumpets, and as it was a lovely sunny day we sat outside for a few minutes.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Two of the kids practised some keepy uppies for a few minutes because on a crazy whim I'd said if they made it to 50 I'd pay them £30 (an attempt to reduce screen time!).</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>Late morning I called my brothers to do the high intensity training workout we have been doing on YouTube each weekday.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>I set up YouTube on my computer and then call them on WhatsApp video, and call out the instructions as we are going along.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>We don't chat much, but it's a chance to check in, and it's nice sibling bonding thing to do the exercise together.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I did the exercise video outside today as the weather was sunny (if a few degrees cooler than it had been a few days ago), and the dog kept bringing me the tennis ball to throw as I was trying to do the workout.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>She's only been allowed one walk a day since lockdown begun seven weeks or so ago.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>She's adapted well and we've given her a long walk - but sometimes she can be a bit persistent wanting the ball continuously thrown around the garden (on the whole though, I think she's loved having everyone at home).</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>After a soup lunch I made, one of the children did some painting in the garden shed he is converting into a recreational space, and the youngest attended a Zoom drama session which she has been doing twice a week with a group of children and a kind drama teacher, who is giving these children an opportunity to let their hair down and socialise.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Some of us did a bit more work/home schooling, and I read some personal emails.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>One was from the primary school headteacher, collecting data to find out how many children would be attending school from the proposed government re- commence date of June 1st.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>There was also an email from the secondary school headteacher, stating that they were going to follow advice this week to try to enable Years 10 and Year 12 (both years sitting major exams next year) to have some school contact before the summer holidays.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>He did say it would be minimal though and that it was unlikely the other year groups would go back until September.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>The letter stated that for the foreseeable future the children's learning would be mainly online.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>This wasn't a surprise but seeing it laid out like this brought reality home.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>There are times when this all seems so enormous, that the sacrifices so many people have to make so big.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>One of the children expressed his disappointment.</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>He was looking forward to socialising with his friends and being the oldest year group on his school site and enjoy the accompanying privileges and the outdoor space in the summer term.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>I think it's really hit home this week that this lockdown with social distancing etc.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>will be here for a long long while and how much it's going to change our lives in the short term.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>How it's going to affect patterns of school, work and how we interact with each other in the future one can only imagine.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>We split up for our one government sanctioned exercise today - Mum cycling and one of the children running.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>The rest of us plus dog went for a walk.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>As we'd just left our house we bumped into some people we'd met on our street for the first time at our VE street party on Friday.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>We discussed how lovely it had been to let our hair down, how even though we'd been social distancing it felt kind of naughty!</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>Had a beautiful walk and saw the mother and father goose with their eight goslings now not so tiny and cute).</RELATIONS> <FOOD>We had a lovely walk together, and I picked wild garlic for making into pesto later.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>We played a game together as we walked - we had to tell each other three 'facts' about ourselves, two of which were true and one false and see if we could guess.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>The dog had fun running around, sniffing wildlife scents, fetching the stick from the river etc..</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>We took some photos of the girls cartwheeling, with the sun going down, which one of the children will use for a photographic fun activity her and her friends have organised: the current theme being 'light'.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>When we got back I gave the cats their wormer and had a cup of green tea and tried to call my friend, who had had to go to hospital with a painful gallstone.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>I wanted to plant some geraniums that another lovely friend had given me, but I ran out of time before supper.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>Mum had made a delicious kedgeree.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>My son had agreed to play Xbox with a friend at a certain time, but don't think they managed to communicate in the end.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>This contact with the children's friends is so important: hanging on to the relationships they had and which hopefully they'll get back to before too long.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>We then wrote our diaries of the day for the Mass Observation Archive.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>The children have got to bed later than I would have liked.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>Hope they are not too fractious tomorrow.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>At least we'll be able to get out for more than one form of exercise from tomorrow, and even have the luxury if we want to of driving a short way to a different walk than the usual routes of the past seven weeks.</ACTIVITIES>

I shower.</ROUTINE> After I <ROUTINE>shower, I decide the tiles in the bathroom are looking a bit dull due to limescale, so I give them a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>liberal spray with Viakal.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I vow to remember to come back and rinse the solution off.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My husband always calls me between 8.15am -8.30am when he is unlocked.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>He rings earlier than</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>usual today at 8.20am, and I race down the stairs to answer the call.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>He always calls my landline</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>phone.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>We can only talk for 10 minutes at a time, so I struggle to remember everything I did since I</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>last spoke to him the day before.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>We’ve been working through some difficult emotions and are sad</EMPLOYMENT> +’t seen each other since 18th March, that was the last time I was able to visit him</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>before lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>Whilst I am on the phone, the landscape gardener arrives to do next-door’s garden.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I remember my</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>neighbours mentioned it to me and I said I was interested in getting a quote, so I end the call with</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>the ‘I love you’ and decide to go outside and breezily ask the chap if he’ll have a look at my garden</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>too.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>He says he’ll pop round and take some measurements and I hope he doesn’t forget because it’s</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>a lot easier to find someone based on recommendation.</ROUTINE> I live in one of the many new-build houses that have been built in Bordon in the last 2-3 years. <ROUTINE>However, the gardens have been done to an appalling standard.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The turf has been laid on top of</ROUTINE> concrete rubble, it is very uneven and the grass has now died. <ROUTINE>Next-door are having theirs levelled</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>and are having astro-turf put down.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I don’t want astro-turf but I would like a better lawn.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I grab a coffee and start my morning calls.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I have a call with my boss Chris and we discuss some of</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>the priorities we are working on.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>As soon as I get off the phone, my husband calls again and he tells me is worried about his Mum as</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>he remembered her dog has been poorly and may have to be put down.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I reassure him that I will call</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>his Mum at lunchtime.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I make some toast for breakfast with butter and marmite.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>And start working at my laptop at the</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>dining room table – my ‘home office’ for this lockdown period.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I usually have the French doors open</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>at the back of my house but the work going on next door is too loud with the digger, so I have to</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>keep shutting the doors.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But I don’t want the chap to forget to give me a quote as I said he could</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>give me a shout over the fence when he was free.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My mother-in-law rings me in tears to tell me she is having her dog Ella, her 14 year old Labrador,</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>put to sleep tomorrow.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Ella has been having fits for several weeks; is wetting the floor and is now no</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>longer eating.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Her quality of life has diminished and Sue tells me she’s spoken to the vet, and they</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>have decided it’s the kindest thing to do.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I offer to go with Sue to the vets to have the dog put to</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>sleep, but Sue explains that because of social distancing the vet will do it in the boot of the car and</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>then Sue can say her goodbyes.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Then the dog will be taken away and cremated.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Even if I was there, I</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>can’t give Sue a hug afterwards.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I have another call at 11.30am with my team, the last couple of weeks we have made it fun by</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>bringing in silly quizzes or wearing stupid hats, but today there isn’t anything like that.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I guess we are</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>all bored of lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>Doesn’t sound as though we’ll be going back to the office anytime soon.</EMPLOYMENT> Maybe not before July at this rate. <ROUTINE>Lunchtime:</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I have a slice of ham and heat-up a tin of chicken and vegetable soup.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’m looking forward to my</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Tesco’s delivery arriving tonight as I haven’t got much in the fridge.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I check to see if my Easyjet flights have been cancelled as I was due to go to Naples next week.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I am</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>relieved to see they now have, but I can’t decide whether to take the voucher they are offering</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>(which is valid for 12 months, could be useful when they start flying again?) or whether to take a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>refund?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I can’t decide.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I share some WhatsApp messages with my sister.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>She lives in Farnborough.</ROUTINE> She has just announced <ROUTINE>she’s pregnant so is having to self-isolate, whilst also being furloghed.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>She’s just baked a banana bread cake.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Afternoon:</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I continue to work on my laptop.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>I was expecting my boss to call me back about something we</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>discussed earlier in the day, but he doesn’t.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I am surprised.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>I finally call him at 4.30pm and he seems</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>to have forgotten what we discussed anyway.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>As I talk to him, I watch the landscape gardener drive away.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Is he coming back tomorrow?</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I do hope</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>so or has he forgotten me after all that!?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My husband calls me for the final time today, 10 mins.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We never struggle of things to talk about but I</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>do hate these 10 min restrictions on our calls.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>I work until 5.30pm on a project that I keep dipping in and out of.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I perform better if I have a</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>deadline to work to and this isn’t an urgent project so I lack the motivation.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>I pack up work for the day and decide to go for a run around the block.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>I am currently doing Couch to</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>5K via an app I downloaded.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>My voice coach is Sarah Milligan and today I am on run 2 of Week 2.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>That’s 5 minutes walk warm-up.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Then 6 x 90 second runs with 2 mins walk in between.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Having run 5K</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>2 years ago, and then given up, I’m surprised my body seems to remember what to do.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I get home and water the plants in the garden and wonder if I will ever get my garden landscaped.</ACTIVITIES> I <ROUTINE>decide if I do get a quote it’s a bonus but to try to chill out about it.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The upheaval of having to ask</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>neighbours to move their cars to get the digger round, the mess and noise it would make and having</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>to worry about being potentially ripped-off seems overwhelming.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Evening:</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I cook a jacket potato for dinner.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I microwave it for 5 mins before cooking in the oven for 45mins, with olive oil and salt. I mix together some tuna, some sweetcorn, some grated gruyere cheese and bind it with some mayonnaise. I load the jacket potato and cook for a further 5 mins.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>I watch the news on TV and a programme about childbirth on BBC.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>At 10pm my Tesco’s delivery arrives, yippee, and I spend 20 mins unloading my shopping.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I am</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>delighted that I got sent 12 eggs for the price of 6 but am disappointed that 2 eggs have cracked and</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>the gin I ordered has leaked slightly.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Still, on balance, less stressful than going to the supermarket.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I Whatsapp my sister again and we try to organise meeting up at the weekend as now the</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>restrictions have been lifted I can walk with her as long as we keep 2m apart.</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>I write a quick email to my husband which I hope he will get tomorrow.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>The emails I send him have been hit and miss recently.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>And that’s my day done.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I spent a lot of time reflecting on what I could have done differently with my life.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Ten years ago I was</ROUTINE> 29 and I thought I had forever in front of me. <ROUTINE>I imagined by the time I got to 39 I would have</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>achieved so much more than I have.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I thought I might have had children for instance and reached</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>that elusive destination of ‘happiness’.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Ten years from now I will be 49, and I just hope I have made peace with my feelings about myself</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>and that I feel finally settled in life.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Life for me has been a series of lurching from one crisis to</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>another and the one good thing about this Coronavirus crisis is that my life is relatively calm for</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>once.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I am enjoying my own company, although I miss my husband and I want him to come home</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>soon.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I’m enjoying my house and my garden and just relishing the simple things in life.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I’m not sure I’m ready for the world to get back to normal again anytime soon.</ROUTINE>

<EMPLOYMENT>I am a 41 year old research manager at a university, living in Cardiff with my husband and</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>3yr old daughter.</RELATIONS> I am currently 7 months pregnant, which should hopefully give you some clues about my gender (female). <ROUTINE>I have been contributing to the Mass observation project for a good number of years now,</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>more than I care to think about, but I think it is the first time I’ve done the May 12th diary.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My husband reminded me about it this morning and I had a rare spare few minutes this</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>evening, so this year is the year I finally make a contribution.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Today has been a fairly typical Tuesday in the new world of lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>My husband and I are</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>both in the vulnerable category (I’m pregnant, he’s asthmatic) so we’ve been self-isolating</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>(ish) for a week longer than most of the population – about 7 weeks I think.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It all rolls into</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>one so is difficult to keep track.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Since my daughter’s school & nursery closed, we’ve been trying to manage on full-time job,</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>one self-employed job and childcare, which has been challenging.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>I don't feel like I’ve had a</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>moments peace in the last 7 weeks.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I’ve either been working, looking onto of my daughter</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>or trying to keep up with life admin.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>We’ve fallen into a bit of a pattern when it comes to childcare / work and Tuesday is one of</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>the days I do a full day.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I was woken up at 6:45 by my daughter coming into our bedroom.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>For the first time in about a week she was actually quite nice to us and didn’t spend the first</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>half an hour shouting at us.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This is a relatively new thing for her to do in the mornings and I</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>think is some kind if phase rather than the result of the lockdown, but I can’t really be sure.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It is difficult for her to understand although she sort of just accepts the situation.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>After being woken up, we go down for breakfast and I have in mind that I might go for a</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>walk before I start work, but that is quickly derailed by my daughter wanting to spend time</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>with me and I can’t really refuse.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>So I get dressed for the day and then join her and her dad</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>playing their Just Dance on the Wii, which has become part of their daily exercise.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>When I’m</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>working, I still dress for work even though I’m now working from home.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>It hadn’t crossed my</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>mind as being an unusual thing to do until I bumped into a friend during the state</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>sanctioned hour of exercise in the park and I was wearing my work clothes and I
