<ROUTINE>outside of Edinburgh.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I work as a school counsellor, and am working from home at the moment because of the virus, but today is not one of my normal working days.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>My partner is not working at the moment as his work could not provide him with remote access, so we are living off just my income currently.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>The day starts at about 7am.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My boy has decided he is awake and wants to go downstairs.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>The two of us go downstairs, leaving my partner asleep in the bed in the spare room.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>Our son has woken up several times in the night and I am tired and feeling resentful that again I am the one to be getting up early yet again, but also know that if I try to go back to bed I won't get any peace anyway.</EMOTIONS> <FOOD>Our boy declares that he wants pancakes for breakfast, and I conclude that we probably have enough milk left for this (we are going shopping roughly every two weeks at the moment in a bid to limit possible virus exposure) and are in no rush to go anywhere so have the time to make them. I make the pancakes and our boy helps at first, but then gets bored and wants me to help him build a play park out of his blocks whilst simultaneously cooking, and I wonder about if its possible he will forget about what play parks are, having not been to one for two months now. I eat my pancakes on the floor whilst we build, and the play park gets turned into a maze which a car gets driven round.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>My partner appears (he was down earlier to make coffee but then vanished again - he did not get any pancakes) and our son tells him to go away (he does this a lot at the moment - its an age and stage thing I think, wanting mummy to himself all the time, or at least I hope.) My partner starts playing on a toy piano and singing tuneless made up songs to our boy and they both become absorbed in doing this and I use this as an opportunity to escape to have a shower on my own.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>When I return they are singing nonsense songs about a downtown snail - I have heard the whole thing from upstairs and it was becoming excruciating to listen to, but our boy still seems to be enjoying it.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>We play a bit more and dither about what to do next, and my partner makes some lunch and I coax the boy into putting some clothes on with a view to going outside.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>We are still potty training him, and have resorted to using bribery and rewards, and the possibility of a lolly is enough to get him to sit on the potty.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>He has a wee and I feel unbelievably proud of him.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>After lunch we go outside, though it is cold and quite windy.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Our boy tries having a go on his scooter, and my partner waters the garden a bit.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Everyone is a bit irritable and the dawdling and indecisiveness lead to a snap decision to go for a walk, though we have walked pretty much everywhere we can go locally.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>We head out for a walk, and observe our neighbour and two friends he doesn't live with all getting out of the same car, seemingly flouting the social distancing rules.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Our walk is a variation on a circular walk through a field of rape we walked round the other day, and tensions mount as we are forced to cross the road several times to avoid pedestrians coming from the other direction.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>We get home and are all tired.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I want to zone out and sit on the sofa and flick idly through my phone, but inevitably this involves my boy coming to join me and demanding videos.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>This gives way into him watching cbeebies on my laptop, mostly his favourite show the Octonauts, even though he has watched it 400 times already this week.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>He watches telly whilst I flick through news stories that I can no longer concentrate on long enough to read properly.</COVIDNEWS> <FOOD>My partner cooks tea (reheated pasta from the other day, with potato salad and bread - all the carbohydrates) and then after tearing him away from the laptop I give our son a bath and do his teeth.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>I read him Walter the Worm from the Mr Men series in bed and he falls asleep quickly.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I lie in bed next to him (he sleeps in our bed, I have yet to work out how to evict him, and he still hasn't got his own room yet.) and send a few messages to friends on WhatsApp.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>Then I read some more news, and get out of bed and come downstairs and write this diary entry.</COVIDNEWS>

<RELATIONS>Me: 39-yr-old white woman, living in Winchester with my male partner and our two children aged 7 ('the boy') and nearly 2 ('the toddler').</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Freelance writer and family covid-19.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>BUT: it's hard.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I'm a freelancer and since the schools and nurseries closed I have had no dedicated time for my work, which keeps me sane as well as earning me money.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>I am mostly looking after the kids while my partner works in his stable, management job where he has to deal with all the furloughing and redundancies that have become necessary.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>We are both worried about the future, like everyone; about everyone who's suffering, the way the government's responding, and especially about our over-70s parents who live 3 hours away and who may not be able to hug their grandchildren for .</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>months?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Years?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>After a broken night up with the toddler, we all got up soon after 7.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>After breakfast we all got dressed, brushed teeth etc before my partner said goodbye and disappeared into the home office (which was the toddler's bedroom until just before lockdown, when we hurriedly bought a desk and then moved her into her brother's room).</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>I played and sang with the toddler while her brother read and did a maths worksheet set by his teacher via Google Classroom - this sounds peaceful but there is a lot of input from me needed whenever the boy does schoolwork, meanwhile the toddler hangs off my knees and demands milk/snacks/songs.</SCHOOL> <EMOTIONS>She's still breastfeeding and it feels like a global crisis is not the moment to take that away from her.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>At 11, as on most weekday mornings these days, we stopped for 'Nana's Art Club', which is a Zoom video call with my mum to do an art activity.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>It's lovely for us all to see each other so regularly but it is hard work - she's hard of hearing and the boy doesn't always listen, so their calls usually need me to act as interpreter, plus there is a lot of paint, glue, paper and so forth to be got out and cleared up and scissors to be moved out of the toddler's reach.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Today we made collages, and then the boy finished filling in the family tree we started together yesterday.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>He seemed genuinely interested, but his main motivation for doing school work at the moment seems to be to earn himself time playing Minecraft.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>After the toddler went to have a nap, my partner joined us for lunch and during the peaceful Minecraft time I made biscuits, cleared up, and caught up on emails.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>Once the toddler woke up, we went for a walk to post a letter.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>There were a few more people out than last week, after Boris Johnson gave a messy speech on Sunday relaxing the lockdown, so we did a few awkward dodges to maintain 2m social distance - walking in the middle of the road to skirt round a group of 3 workmen chatting close together, and going up someone else's garden path to let a couple of dog walkers go past.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>So many major and trivial parts of everyday life will be hard to navigate as lockdown 'eases', and it's incredibly worrying at the moment that the government is being so unclear about what's going on.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>It feels as though we're being urged to use our 'common sense' and get the economy going again, without enough information on how the virus is transmitted, how prevalent it is and where.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Whenever cabinet ministers try to clarify things they seem to be mentioning golf courses, tennis and nannies - and not, for instance, addressing the problems of social distancing in overcrowded schools made worse by staff shortages as many teachers are in, or live with people in, high risk groups.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Anyway, we posted our letter and came home again.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>I made dinner dhal, flatbreads, broccoli and fishfingers - not all things were eaten by all people) and then had a video chat with two local friends while my partner got the kids ready for bed.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>It's nice to see my friends' faces, but a 40-minute call just scratched the masks in crowded public places - so as I have a sewing machine and spare suitable fabric, I'm planning to make some as soon as I get time.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>At the start of lockdown everyone in I am a 51 year old married man with two teenage daughters. <EMPLOYMENT>I live in Glasgow and work as a consultant engineer in the oil industry.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Today I woke up early, at 6:30, and went for a walk in the woods with my wife. Update on the telly.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>I try to steer clear of the news these days.</COVIDNEWS> The <COVIDNEWS>BBC is erring towards propaganda and away from hard-hitting journalism these days.</COVIDNEWS> <FOOD>A concoction of leeks, chicken, cream and past for tea, then my wife and daughter run the weekly Guides meeting</FOOD> <DIGITAL>(via Zoom again).</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>I hide in the bedroom and play synthesisers.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>This is one of my peculiarities.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I like to mess around making appalling music on the laptop.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>The key rules are that the software I use has to be free, or very cheap, and</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>that nobody gets to hear the output.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>As far as I can tell, others who do this sort of thing I am <RELATIONS>23 years old, female, single, and have been living in my childhood home just outside Southampton with my parents since I graduated from university two years ago.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I work for xxxxxx Council (xxx) as an administrative assistant (my first real job!), where the bulk of my job is creating and issuing contractual documentation for local government employees, including school staff.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>I have been working from home for nearly two months now because my team starting sending employees home before the official countywide lockdown began on 23rd March 2020.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>This 12th May is unlike any other, and yet it’s already become a routine – so much so that it’s easy to forget what day of the week it is, just that it’s a work day.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>My day began just after 7.30am when my mum woke me for the day. We don’t get in trouble if we lag behind; we’re more likely to get a concerned supervisor ask if everything’s okay and if we’re having trouble with something.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>My productivity used to be way above the 32 guideline. My brother is playing the piano, and then a Radiohead song, alternately.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>My mum lies on the sofa, recovering, reading books and buying things online obsessively, even though she knows how bad Amazon is.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Capitalist, neoliberal nightmare.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I told her about the bottle of Amazon worker urine in the Museum of Neoliberalism.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>She was disgusted but didn't consider changing her habits.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My dad sits in his study, practising a Pink Floyd solo on guitar and programming his LED lights to do cool dances when he plays songs, like Pictures of You by The Cure.</RELATIONS> The past decade has been so swift and so automatic. <SCHOOL>Going to university was a reflex, an end goal.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>I went to the wrong university, and I regret it, but good opportunities have come from it.</SCHOOL> But now? <EMOTIONS>Now I am terrified, aimless.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>The government seem to be children.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>The planet is dying.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The systems are fizzling out, there are no adults, nobody is taking responsibility.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>There is vitriolic public shaming, virtue signalling, anger, there is love and compassion but it is scattered, ununited, piecemeal, fragmented.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>They say every generation has a different reason for thinking the world is going to end, but this time it is something that is within our control, only we don't seem to be stopping it.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>In terms of COVID-19, me and my brother have stopped using shampoo to get our hair to recondition itself, but mine is just disgusting.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I also cut my hair myself, which was quite silly - it's now a bob, a very bad one.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>That stuff has never really bothered me though.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I've been running, using the NHS OneYou Couchto5k app, always in straight lines so I can avoid all the inclines I'd face if I diverged from the path.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>I've been reading so much - I find TV depressing and overwhelming, it's too much noise.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I seem to be hypersensitive.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My brother will walk into a room and touch me on the shoulder, or say hello, and it makes me jump and I want to snap at him.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>There are not enough bedrooms in our house, so he gives me his room every time I come home.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>He sleeps next to my mum and my dad sleeps in the living room.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>She has had mental health problems for so many years that it's natural for my little brother to want to be close to her.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>She's always been lovely, throughout it all.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I think this time off from life is doing her good.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It's restorative.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>My boyfriend is pretty angry/upset that I chose to go to my family instead of to him on lockdown, but they've always needed me and my mum was so anxious at me being far from home during in a pandemic.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>How could I refuse?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I will always have to be there, when they need me.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Most of all, I am sad that I have all this time and no plan for the future and yet I still don't write, which is all I ever want to do.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I've had some kind of writers' block for 8 years, since I got distracted by boys when I was 16, and it's breaking my heart.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>How do I get past it?</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Am I even a writer at this point?</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I do write - but it's so scattered and not collected anywhere and I never work on anything longer than the first sitting, when I write it.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>And I can never share it.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>So, thank you to this project for getting me to write.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Sorry it's so long.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The way things are - it's not really okay.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>And nobody will say it, or nobody will listen.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Myself included.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It's scary, the trajectory we're on, how hard it seems to be to put on the breaks.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>We're approaching a precipice of some kind.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>But when we fall in, things don't go away - we aren't absolved by oblivion.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It's going to be slow, and there will be a lot of time for reflection.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>And what we must learn most of all is not to blame ourselves to the point of inertia.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>Every day we have another change.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Every second.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>If things are irreparably bad, we do what we can, we love each other all the same.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We do our best.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I am rooting for you, planet earth.</EMOTIONS>

<RELATIONS>My name is , I am aged 22 and I was born with Down Syndrome (my mum is typing this for me!).</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>I am visually impaired and I go to a College for students with additional needs where I study independent living skills, English and Maths.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>My tutor thought I might be interested in recording today's diary.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>We are in total lockdown due to the Covid 19 virus and I have not left the house in the last 8 weeks.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>My mum and my sister are both working from home and I am studying at home with the help of my mum.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>College regularly sends me work packs to complete.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Today I got up, had my bath and my breakfast of Weetabix and banana, mum put in my contact lens and I had my hay fever medicine.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I put on my ID badge ready to sit at the table to do my College work at 9am.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>This morning I carried out a science experiment, I made a boat out of paper that floated on water and kept adding buttons to it until it sank.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>Then I weighed the buttons.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>I fed my 8 tortoises with salad and greens, emptied the dishwasher and made tomato soup with warm bread rolls for lunch for my family and then washed up.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>This afternoon I filled the bird feeders in the garden, and filled the bird bath and drinking bowl for the foxes and badgers with water.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I planted flower seeds in the green house and put tomato plants and other plants into bigger pots.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>For tea I had fish fingers with salad and a bowl of strawberries and blueberries with yoghourt for pudding.</FOOD> <DIGITAL>In my free time in the evening I like to speak to my boyfriend and friends on my mobile phone and use my iPad and watch TV in my bedroom.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I have a bath and a drink of milk before bed.</ROUTINE>

text: <ROUTINE>This diary is anonymous, and while intended to be used for research, not intended to be linked to me.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>So I will say what I think and feel of the past few years and the next decade - and it is, on balance, not a completely happy picture.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Covid is at least not man made, unlike many of the other problems in our society.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Oddly enough, there is a comfort in that.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We face this crisis together.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It has in fact brought our local community together.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But it has also widened cracks in our society and increased dangers for the future.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>And some of that is deeply worrying -- to an extent that I occasionally doubt our human society has a future at all.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>A brief self portrait: your age, where you live, your relationship status, your present job or occupation if you are working and any other information that you think is important to record.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>both of whom are married, and doing well professionally.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Our beautiful grandson was born earlier this year.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I practice as a lawyer, and am a partner in an international firm.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My wife and I enjoy hiking in nature, adventure travel, art, and beekeeping.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>She paints and photographs.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>Personally, I love maritime history, sailing tall ships as a trainee, and classical music (including the ethereal beauty of the English polyphony).</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>Any reflections on the day and on how you felt while keeping the diary are welcome.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>You can also reflect on the past decade and look forward to the next ten years.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Today started like any other lockdown day.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I woke up at 7.30, looked outside into the quiet street (sunny again, with a tinge of worry that we don't get enough rain), and brushed teeth.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>I had no dreams, indeed have had very few since Covid-19, while I usually have very vivid and varied dreams.</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>We lie in bed reading the online papers and personal emails for an hour, drinking our morning tea.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>We try not to let the news depress us, as difficult as it is, and we discuss a few items that pique our interest.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>I can say I am deeply grateful for my wife of 35 years, who is lovely and keeping me together.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>She is an introvert and secretly enjoying lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>We are well suited in that she is much wiser than I am, and that keeps me steady.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>There was one item of beauty amongst the otherwise dreary news today: our church choir, who are all excellent singers, circulated an online performance pieced together from individual voices each singing in isolation.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It's a piece of Renaissance music I had not heard before.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>But a ray of beauty in a worrying time is a relief.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Another interesting thing I read this morning was an editorial in the FT, entitled "The wealthy should welcome an opportunity to reduce inequality; Covid-19 has thrown the gap between the rich and not-rich into sharp focus - the time has come to take action". I thought that was very timely, and a bit of a surprise coming from the FT.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I posted a pseudonymous comment, which I copy here, since it reflects my view of society and politics -- FWIW: Every just society combines three elements of fairness: equality, equity, and reciprocity (or solidarity).</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Equality in that the basic needs of every individual should be met, which include water, food shelter, safety, healthcare and education; Equity in the sense that every individual and group should be able to earn a profit from hard work and intelligence (and even luck) whether as entrepreneurs or otherwise; and Reciprocity in that everyone should give back to society in accordance with their means and ability.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Due to various economic factors (internationalization and digitization of the economy) inequality appears to be on the increase -- and appearances are as important as reality.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>A crisis like the 2008 financial upheaval or this year s Covid-19 shows us that the balance may have to be recalibrated: those who can should support those in need.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>And if we who can don't follow and do this, the recalibration will be involuntary.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>The problem with voluntary calibration is the age-old coordination problem: why should l contribute if my equally rich neighbour doesn't?</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>He would benefit and free ride on my solidarity.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>There are various solutions to that, ranging from persuasion (like "The Giving Pledge" campaign to encourage extremely wealthy people to contribute a majority of their wealth to philanthropic causes).</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>So far just over 200 billionaires and millionaires have signed up.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>That is far away on the long tail, so we need more.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>And if voluntary means do not help, taxation is a solution to ensure the pain is spread fairly (in accordance with means).</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>lf we ignore this, the outcome could be civil unrest and destruction.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>So, voluntary and State led solutions are preferable.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>But we are dreaming if we think we can ignore this I have a shower and go off to work around 8.45 am.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>The commute, which used to take 35 minutes by tube into the City, now takes 35 seconds walking down the stairs to get behind my desk.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>We have a large partners desk in the study, so my wife and I sit opposite each other while we work.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>We have a simple breakfast while working (yoghurt, nuts, and fruit).</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>My wife writes her book or does research, or paints in her little studio in the attic.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>I dive into my emails and my first Zoom call.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>I have clients in various time zones, so there is a pile of emails waiting every morning, and the email tsunami can last to late evening.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Never a moment s rest, often including large chunks of the weekend.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>We take lunch and tea at our desk, and my wife takes some time to potter about in the house.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>My work goes on until 7.30 pm today, so not too late.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>One of the calls is with my partners, to discuss the strategy we should follow for the London office in these difficult days.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Work from home started early March, and everyone had the necessary IT tools -- we allowed work from home even before the lockdown so long as it did not affect clients or colleagues.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>With the lockdown, a conscious effort is made to stay in contact, with twice weekly practice group calls in the London office, a twice weekly partners' call to keep track of developments, and strategize.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Add to that regular client calls, ongoing project management calls, individual calls with team members to check everyone is OK, and anywhere between 100 to 250 emails a day, and a day is quickly filled.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Working from home is actually quite efficient, and little time is wasted, although I miss the occasional impromptu chats with colleagues and clients.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>There is little doubt that even after Covid-19 has passed, we will work from home more often The immediate future is a worry for us, though.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>The pipeline of new transactions is mostly empty.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>I have a few ongoing projects, a looming arbitration, and ongoing advice for worried clients that keeps me fully occupied now, but who knows whether that will keep up.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Some practice groups are very busy, like dispute resolution, whereas others see their work fall off a cliff.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>They dive into business development or try to retrain for the wave of restructuring or bankruptcies that will probably follow, or help colleagues.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>The atmosphere in the team is excellent, and mutually supportive, but I notice concerns and stress below the surface.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Clients delay payment to hold on to their cash, or demand discounts.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>Revenues are down and the partners are the first to feel that.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>We can forget profit distributions this year, so will be digging into pension savings (which I realize we are lucky to have). In the meantime, we have to keep paying salaries and ongoing expenses. Unlike some other firms, we have no debt and can survive on our capital reserves for six months or so.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Some other firms have had capital calls already (partners having to pay to work instead of being paid), so we can count ourselves lucky for the time being, but a second Covid-19 wave and new lockdown in the fall or winter could be a problem.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>The partnership have lots of discussions whether we should cut costs, put people on furlough, or cut salaries.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>The prudent ones argue we should do so to prepare for prolonged recession and take action now to preserve the business in the long run.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>Others point at a reputational risk, or argue that we seniors who enjoyed the upside last year should now accept the pain.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>The latter camp has the upper hand for the time being, and I take pride in my colleagues' attitude.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>But it's scary and the responsibility keeps me awake at night: the teams rely on us to keep their families fed, and while institutions like ours are built over many decades, this crisis brings home that it can all collapse in a very short time.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>Perhaps my worry (and, if I am honest, anger) is really about the state of the world.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Covid-19 is a natural phenomenon.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>There is a lingering thought that humanity's rape of nature has brought it about, although pandemics have been with us at least since the beginning of organized society, agriculture and trade.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But what is happening in the UK and worldwide is man-made, and deeply damaging.</ROUTINE> Take Brexit, which everyone now ignores. <DIGITAL>I chose the law as my calling all those years ago because of what happened to my family and others during WWII.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Europe needs to work together and build institutions based on the rule of law, and create a society based on peace, equality, equity, and solidarity.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>For me, the EU was a symbol of that.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>An imperfect one, and sometimes annoyingly so, because every human endeavour is imperfect.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But it was one of the great politically visionary experiments of mankind, together with the US Constitution (now under threat due to Trump).</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>And we are crashing out of the EU because of a combination of greed (hedge funds like Odey pay a million to Brexit campaign while shorting the pound, making a tidy £220 million overnight), and naked political ambition (ranging from selfish clown Johnson to repulsive Francois and Farage, libertarian ideologues, and that Rasputin Cummings).</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>Add to that the malice of a dismal tabloid press, manipulating a public that is part gullible and short-sighted and part frustrated by being left behind by austerity.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>A toxic mix.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>The government is using the pandemic as a convenient distraction while pursuing their goal of a hard Brexit.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>And of course, they will blame the EU in case some silly soul complains.) The combined economic impact of Brexit and Covid will be awful.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>I have done what I can campaigning, and I try to hide it my anger because no one wants to hear about it anymore, but I am still livid and depressed about the political vandalism.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>We are abandoning and helping to destroy the international institutions we will need in the future.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>What happens in the UK is a worldwide trend, of an ongoing deflation and erosion of democracy, and the growth of extremism and dictatorships.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Some may have "just" local effects, like in Poland, Hungary, and India.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>But some have worldwide consequences, like in Russia, China, the United States and Brazil, who are working to undermine the values (such as they are) of the West, and who are actively frustrating work to stop climate change.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>And that leads me to the worst prospect that is being ignored during the Covid crisis: climate change.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>We should be working now to switch to a green economy, and move away from mindless consumption.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>Instead, everyone is distracted by Covid-19.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I know we personally have no reason to complain, since we haven't suffered (yet) like others whose houses were flooded or burned -- even the Arctic burnt last summer!</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Yet we are starting to feel it ourselves.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>From my desk, I see our empty bee hives.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Our bee colonies have not survived the early spring.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>We (my wife and I and two experienced beekeeping friends with a network of beekeepers to share information and experience) have done everything by the book.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Our bees went into the winter healthy and with sufficient stores of honey we had left them. The Varroa mite treatment around new year was successful. When an unusually warm February broke, we saw the bees coming out, and young bees learning to fly in front of the hives, hovering around the hive entrance with their legs dangling as young bees do. But then their numbers dwindled. During the two-weekly inspections we saw fewer and fewer bees. There was honey and even pollen, but the queens were not laying new eggs.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>Last week, we saw the last lonely bee.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>Devastating.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>It is unclear what happened, but it seems the bees broke the winter cluster too soon, during the (too early) warm days, and then suffered when it became cold again.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>No signs of illness, and neither we nor (we think) our neighbours use pesticides in the gardens -- so we think it is an effect of irregular weather patterns, possibly due to climate change.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>We are not alone: our local beekeepers' association lost most of their bees, and others report colony collapse too.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>Our last, sad bee during our hive inspection last weekend We are not giving up.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>We have ordered new queens for June, and have set up a bait hive, to see whether we can catch a swarm.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>We read that so many hives are unattended during the lockdown that more colonies will split and swarm, and the papers this weekend reported a few large swarms scaring people.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>It is hard, however, not to see this and Covid-19 as a sign and symbol of a possible collapse of society and nature as we know it.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>We contribute financially to charities fighting climate change, and I stick out my neck professionally by writing and speaking, arguing that we have the legal tools to change things.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>But these are words blown away by the wind of indifference.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>A voice crying in the wilderness.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>I fear a looming disaster, and that Covid-19 will be child's play compared to what is coming.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Not that all is bad in the short term.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>The lockdown has really brought our local community together.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>My wife and a neighbour set up a WhatsApp group for mutual support and to maintain a feeling of togetherness.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>This has worked really well -- almost everyone in our street and even around has joined, and is sharing supportive, witty, and thoughtful comments, and sharing Ocado deliveries.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>The local vicar and his wife are great, organizing food and assistance for Age UK and the local hospice, and holding services online.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>Our wonderful church choir is occasionally singing online (although performing artists have a hard time, and it isn't the same as live music), the air is clear, we hear the birds, traffic is light, and everyone is friendly at a responsible distance.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>Another good news item: I received a message from "our" refugee this afternoon, who is in lockdown while fasting during Ramadan.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>He is originally from Somalia, and has a horrid past being shot by Al Shabaab, and fleeing through Libya, across the Mediterranean, and via the jungle of Calais, to the UK.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>He saw rape and killing.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Now that he is here, he is not allowed to work or study and is wasting the most productive years of his life due to the "hostile environment".</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>We had him in our house for a few months a while ago, via "refugees at home", until he moved on to another host.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>He knocked on our door at the beginning of the lockdown since he had lost his shelter, but we could not take the risk of taking him in -- he said he was sleeping on public transport, and could have been anywhere and be contaminated.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>(My wife was concerned I had been speaking with him too closely and too long, not knowing whether he could be contagious, and banned me to the guest room for more than a week since she cannot afford to catch it - another vignette from Covid-19 days.) We gave him some money for food and arranged a simple hotel room for a few nights.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Remarkably, we found a small room for him via a neighbouring council who was taking in the homeless.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>We made a good donation to St Mungo's and Streetlink but frankly, we both feel awful and selfish for not taking him in.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>We're buying off our guilt to keep our health.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>After the last video conference meeting, at 7.30, we have a quick dinner - neither of us are into cooking or fine dining so we keep it simple.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>We were about to go for our evening walk when my wife remembered that we have our photographic society meeting" together at 8. Usually we meet in the church undercroft
column7: with members showing and talking about their photographs.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My wife occasionally wins contests.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Tonight was someone speaking about photography and "the Sublime". Truly gorgeous photographs in the style of Ansel Adams of awesome
column9: and dangerous nature.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>It's odd to think that the greatest danger we are experiencing now comes from an invisible tiny enemy.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>The evening ended with Whatsapp photos showing our grandson growing.</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>He is now four months old
column10: and a happy and healthy child.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>He may be lucky that he has both his parents with him all day
column11: giving him undivided attention - in between their work.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>He seems to thrive.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>A good way to end the day.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Conclusion: Reflecting on the day leaves me with mixed feelings.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>Gratitude for my wife and family
column12: for the lovely community in which I live
column13: and for the fact that we are healthy
column15: and have no financial worries.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>It's mostly luck
column16: and we try to share it out.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>But what I see happening in the UK and worldwide is depressing - destruction of nature
column17: and erosion of democracy and institutions we need to overcome the challenges that are awaiting us.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Covid-19 is just a passing phase.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Extremists shooting up a maternity hospital tonight (on the news tonight).</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>I feel our society and humanity are sliding in the abyss.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Is it the looming sense of my own mortality now that I am past 60?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I think not.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>I don't fear death
column18: from Covid or otherwise - my father had a near-death experience and found it beautiful and uplifting.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>But the mindless and selfish destruction of what is beautiful
column22: tinged with anger.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>This photo from this evening's presentation reflects the mood.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>It feels like the end of times.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>IMAGE 8 IMAGE 9</ROUTINE>"

text: <COVIDNEWS>Dear diary, This is our third month in quarantine.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>Last night at 3am did exercise and then showered, I then played Animal Crossing: New Horizons.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>The music on AC (animal crossing) is different at both 3am and Sam, I really like the Sam music it is very calming. I have been spending a lot of time on Animal Crossing and Minecraft during quarantine to take my mind off of everything going on.</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>Lockdown has been really stressful because although I have all the time in the world, it still feels that there aren't enough hours in the day to do what I want to do as well as what I HAVE to do.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>My autism, depression and anxiety have made this an especially troubling time with the lack of routine, structure and support that would usually come with education.</EMOTIONS> <SCHOOL>Having to bring education into my home and safe place has also been something I've struggled with but I'm getting more and more used to it and at peace with it as time goes on, as someone who does a practical and creative course, doing this from home has presented its struggles, however, with the ongoing community support and engagement lots of resources have been made free for students to access and use in these trying times.</SCHOOL> <EMOTIONS>Today specifically was a lazy day other than writing this diary entry, I am happy CSEs have been cancelled but im displeased with the government announcement from yesterday, I am concerned of a second spike of covid-19 occurring which would then cause a second lockdown and quarantine, probably for an even longer time.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>I just want to enjoy my summer without my friends dying and I'm not very hopeful of it.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>This will bring lots of changes into the world and it already has, the way we act as a society needs to change and we all need to pull together to get through this.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Unfortunately, some members of the public feel they are invincible to the disease, making it harder on the rest of us who are actually taking it seriously.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I'm hoping some positives might be that the N S get a pay rise and key workers and front-line workers do too.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Hygiene standards are changing as well with enforced sanitising in almost all business places and even in our homes, shopping routines have changed, basically everything we once knew 3 months ago is very different.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>That's all I have to say today.</ROUTINE> Bye diary,

<SCHOOL>A fifty-year-old male librarian living in Norwich, married to a university professor (M*) with two children under 10 years old (F* and G*).</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>We also have my wife's elderly parents living in our house.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>*Have changed their initials to help preserve anonymity.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>The day began at midnight because I was still up working, preparing a presentation for a workshop I'm contributing to this Saturday for PhD students.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>This was going to be a face-to-face event, but is now being run virtually.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>I got to bed around 1:00, but was not sufficiently relaxed to sleep straight away and so watched two episodes of the BBC's adaptation of Normal People, which has really engaged me and cast me in a sentimental, slightly melancholy frame of mind over the past couple of days since I started watching it.</DIGITAL> <EMOTIONS>Has brought back many thoughts of early loves and roads not taken.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I spent the night in our spare room so as not to wake M when I went to bed, but we were both awoken at 5:30 by three-year-old G crashing into our bedroom.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>After two cups of strong expresso coffee, and a quick check of my email, I took our dog out for a walk.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>We live on a major road and even at 6:00 it's strange to be able to cross the road without any sign of cars or trucks or people moving.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>This is one aspect of the lock-down that I enjoy and will miss - the empty streets.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>Back home at 7:00 in time to catch the headlines on Radio 4's Today programme, another two shots of coffee, and the beginning of this diary.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Moved quickly to working - important to snatch as much time as I can before the others get up and my day becomes more disrupted.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>Half an hour of emails before shaving my head and beard (felt so much better for it - luckily, given current crisis, I shave it myself every week or two rather than relying on hairdressers - haven't been to one for 15 years and grateful to have a shorter cut now that my hair is turning silver).</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I noticed in the bright morning light that the bathroom needs a clean - should try to do this soon.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>We had a cleaner who came in for three hours once a week but mutually agreed to stop this with the lock-down (paid her a month's cleaning to help her out).</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>I then began the slow wakening up of older daughter (F) who is always hard to get out of bed.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Younger daughter (G) tends to spend the early hours of the day, as she was today, watching cartoons on Netflix beside her mother in bed, busy answering emails.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>Breakfast 8:30-9:00. F had cornflakes and G unusually had eggs in cups) with me - in need of using up as they were just past use-by date.</FOOD> <SCHOOL>I read G a story and then we watched a video morning greeting from her preschool teacher.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>A third cup of coffee with some emails and then I joined our morning catch-up meeting, 10:00-10:30 on Microsoft Teams with work colleagues.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>Broke off to check in with older daughter F and steer her towards some of the more productive exercises in her Google Classroom.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>Her school (a private girls' school) have heavily programmed their home schooling, with videos from teachers, several assignments to hand in each day, and regular virtual meet-ups with teachers and classmates.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>Home schooling is supposed to be done between 9:00 and 3:00, but it regularly takes us 'till supper time to tick off the assignments, and even then it requires a lot of supervision and some of the work is pretty roughly done.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>We've had a talk with her teacher (by skype last week) about the workload and F's behaviour, which has become quite bad at times and is being blamed on us or excused by the lock-down situation.</SCHOOL> <DIGITAL>I hung out our washing (a lovely sunny morning) and then had a cup of tea at 11am and then tackled some emails and this diary entry.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>At 11:30, I signed G into her Google Meet so she could attend the nursery storytime and wave to her friends - something she only occasionally engages fully in.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>Today, she wasn't really paying any attention to the story or screen but I still locked up my computer for 25 minutes while this was going on!</DIGITAL> <FOOD>Lunch after that, 12:00-1:00, with G refusing to eat the food she'd asked me to make (tortellini with green pesto) and only after an hour beginning to pick away at some sandwiches which she'd rejected yesterday. The older girl, F, is similarly fussy but I managed to satisfy her with a bowl of tinned spaghetti hoops covered in cheese. I had left-overs: last night's veggie bolognaise we're a vegetarian family).</FOOD> <DIGITAL>F was dragged off her iPad (skype and computer game with a couple of friends) at 1:00 and returned her to maths under the supervision of her mum.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>G sat and watched some Cbeebies while picking at more food - tomatoes and orange - before I got her upstairs around 1:45 for a story and a nap.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>We don't usually allow her a nap any more but she's been waking increasingly early and very tired in the afternoons, so we thought it might help today.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Probably a bad idea.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>She slept 2:10- 2:45 and I took the opportunity to walk around the block to a Tesco Express supermarket and pick up some milk.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>A big mistake - a queue and a slow walk around the shop.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Taped lines to keep people apart and arrows to indicate how they should walk around the store.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Self-service machines screened from each other with cardboard and the serviced tills screened with perspex.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Back home in time to join the staff briefing from the Library Director at 3:00; again using Microsoft Teams.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>About 40 colleagues on this call - all but one or two muted and with cameras off.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Interactions and banter confined to the chat.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>The briefing took about half an hour and focused on preparations for reopening the Library, which are still fairly vague and unlikely to happen until 27 May at the earliest - and even then probably just a no-contact 'click and collect' service with no human contact and minimal staff on the building.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>There followed a couple of hours of interrupted working and child-minding, with too much reliance on screens.</EMPLOYMENT> <FOOD>Then I put on some rice and M repurposed last night's pasta sauce into a curry.</FOOD> <EMPLOYMENT>Nine-year-old F finally finished her school work around 5.30 and then had a couple of hours of screen-time with her two friends, broken all too briefly, by our supper.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Little G dragged hers out longer, finally having something to eat around 7:00 and then announcing that she wanted to go to bed.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>All too early - especially given her afternoon nap.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Once she'd changed into her pyjamas and brushed her teeth and said goodnight to her grandparents it was nearly 7:30, but took an hour and a half of reading and coaxing to get her off to sleep.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>She can now hug her grandparents goodnight.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>They kept up a few weeks of isolation within our house, begun even before the official lockdown, but relaxed this late last week.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>Due in part, I think, to boredom and in part a feeling that they needed to be helping us out more as we were quite obviously struggling with the children, our demanding jobs and having to do extra cleaning and shopping for them.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>We haven't fully reaped the benefits of this yet, but it will be important to be able to call on them if this drags on for many more months and if we are partially back at work and the kids partially back at school.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>The little one, G, didn't fully understand why her grandparents were keeping themselves away from us and she was no longer allowed to go into certain rooms in the house, so it's more straightforward now that we'll all a 'bubble' together.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>M took our older daughter out to walk the dog, getting home quite late (9:40) and with F in a bit of a state.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>Something had reminded her of her grandparents' dog, which had died when she was four or five and has become a touchstone when she wants to tap into grief.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>So the going to bed was interrupted by a search for the dog's toy, retained as a relic, and a great deal of sobbing.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I think she must has dropped off about 10:15.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>This emotional outburst and the increasing anger during the day must, I think, be partly due to the strange circumstances we're now in.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>The feelings of loss and constraint.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>G has created some imaginary "South American friends" to project her concerns onto.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>She is worried that her friends are unwell and are not able to get a message through or come and visit us.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>Her other grandparents my parents) live in New Zealand and we've increased the online contact during this time (using Zoom app).</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>We are booked to visit them this Summer, but that trip doesn't seem likely to be going ahead now - although M is toying with the idea of achieving this if we can, even if it means two weeks of quarantine in NZ and another couple on our return.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>The airline is refusing us a refund and she's worried that it will be so much more difficult and expensive to travel there in the future.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>M and I watched a bit of Unorthodox (a powerful Netflix drama based on the true story of an ultra-orthodox woman's escape to Berlin) and then I left her to do some work on my laptop downstairs - to complete this diary entry, attend to a couple of urgent emails, and do some more preparation for my Saturday workshop.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>It has just passed midnight and I expect to be up for another hour or so.</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>Taking a slightly different tack in today's blog as today (12 May) is the day that the Mass Observation Archive is asking for diaries capturing lives of everyday people in the UK, so I thought I'd contribute.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>For the record: Self Portrait of an Auditor Age/Sex: 36 / Male Habitat: Lewisham Borough, Southeast London Relationship: Married with 2 kids Occupation: Auditor Working: Yes (from home!) Nationality: Swedish So those are the official formalities.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>This blog is the 58th day in a row I've written down my thoughts, starting with the Monday where the office said to work from home, which was 4 days before the country was told to go into full lockdown as part of the Coronavirus measures enacted by the UK government.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>In those 57 previous days I've written down some thoughts on the day, musings, and often a poem to commemorate how I feel.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>It's how I've been able to cope with the mental strain of living through a global pandemic, and today is no different.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Almost.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>The thing that sets the 12th of May apart from everything and anything else is that it is my younger daughter's birthday.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>And if you think that celebrating a child's birthday is hard in normal times when you arrange play dates or a small birthday party (why not rent a bouncy castle, we've thought in the past, what's the worst that can happen...</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Crying children.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>That's what can happen) arranging to have a birthday in the times of Coronavirus is outright difficult and challenging as anything.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>The friends playing are replaced by Facetime calls with family members and social distance hellos.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Young kids are not fantastic at realizing how important it is to actively engage in these sessions, but at least we got our daughter to engage well with a few grandparents and uncles on facetime, even for just 3-4 minutes.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>She wasn't really all that bothered, really what she wanted was to play with all of us in the house instead.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Still, it was good to have some interaction with someone outside the house by facetime.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>We also managed the new "normal" of socializing by social distancing.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>3 of her friends came to our house, stood at the gate about 2.5m away to sing happy birthday and drop off some gifts.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Which admittedly given that we don't spray things we get down when we get them or wipe them down with some kind of super-gel (anti-bac stuff has to be strong to kill viruses) might defeat the point.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>But still, we got visitors which raised my daughter's spirts a good deal.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>That's how birthdays have to be for now.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>The good news is that even at 2.5 meters and without the hugs you normally want, it was a cheery sight.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>And it was a lot cheaper to have waving at my gate replace bouncy castles, or soft-play parties, or some kind of McDonalds super-takeaway party that has been the norm in years past with both younger and older daughters.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>The day itself was surprisingly tiring.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>For me, as with many days, the issue with the new Work From Home (WFH) normal is that so much of what I do is now dependent on Skype meetings.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Today was no exception, with meetings 10-12, 12:30-5 with one 30 minute break.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>So it meant the day effectively was to be woken up, or arguably pounced on, at 6:50 AM to get dressed so we can rush down and do presents, and then playing till 10, then doing work in those meetings, having a stop at 5 to do more play and get dinner ready, put both kids to bed, then go back to doing work 8PM-11PM (have called it there for today to write this blog and try and get to bed this side of midnight because tomorrow is 2 very important meetings) to try and get back on top of the work that I can't do during endless Skype sessions.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>The good news is, because we did presents in the morning, the kids actually had a lot of stuff to play with.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>New sticker books, new board game, new video game, a Ukulele.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>It did however mean that the Home-schooling that we are supposed to do every day really didn't happen today.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>All the other days we usually get at least 1-2 hours of good productivity out of the younger one, and probably similar for the older one.</SCHOOL> <ACTIVITIES>But today was pure birthday play and birthday joy.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Although not entirely without the odd tangle or issue.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>For example, it takes quite a toll to keep trying to keep the kids entertained, and my wife admirably baked a birthday cake from scratch, partly yesterday and partly today.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>Present it to my daughter who said "wow that's nice" but then once set on the table said "Next year, my friends mom can make it because hers was bigger" which obviously wasn't meant in spite, but sent my wife to the kitchen in tears after hours slaving trying to bake something and craft it the way we'd been told she wanted it.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>As I said, not without a hitch.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My wife knows nothing really was meant by it, but it's hard anyway when you try your best and the kids don't get how hurtful a throwaway comment can be.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>For me, the biggest joy was that the birthday dinner wish was in fact to order a takeaway. Something we have tried not to go TOO overboard with given that we probably could afford one every other day or more. Currently limiting our takeaway consumption to twice a week, once midweek and once Saturday, as a way of engendering local businesses, saving us a night of cooking, and just as a treat. Today we therefore made a takeaway day.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>No cooking required.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>London has definitely brought out the convenience of big city living in the times of a pandemic in that regard.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>We've self-isolated 2 times for 14 day periods, and it has little impact because you can get takeaways, shopping deliveries from actual supermarkets who deliver to your door, and now there's even on-the-day deliveries like a takeaway where they go to local shops and bring you stuff.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I don't even care that it's got delivery fees, tips, processing fees and then no deals on your shopping, it's just a safe mechanism to ensure we always have the ability to get food in if we are so inclined.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>As a result, the last time I went to a shop was 53 days ago, and I have not missed it one bit.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>This despite the fact that the normal for the better part of 4 years since I started my current job is to pop to the shop at the point where we notice something is missing and really not being that well-planned out in our approach.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Convenience rules.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>The evening routine was bedtime stories, then as she's now old enough, youngest child joining her older sister to sleep in her own room in her bunkbed.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>In truth we've been trying to get her in there for many months leading up to this and every night around 12-2AM she comes walking in and plonks herself between my wife and I in our room.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I have every confidence that magically turning 5 won't change this, but I remain to be impressed on this fact tonight.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Shall see what happens.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>The night then has been reading committee papers, reviewing some work done on a couple of my audits, and preparing for my section in the meeting tomorrow.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Because tomorrow is another hell-on-earth day.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>2.5 hour committee session in the morning, followed by 3 hour session in the afternoon, with a couple extra meetings sprinkled in for good measure.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>The WFH arrangements work really well in audit as everything can be done online, it's just surprising how drained you feel at the end of it all.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Given the prompt, I thought I'd post a few reflections on the decade gone and decade ahead, accepting this is a fairly brief format to do so.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>2010-2020 2010 was when I first became an accountant, joining a major audit firm and training there.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>It's marked a decade as an auditor, in which the audit profession has advanced in leaps and bounds technologically in trying to use data analytics, machine learning, continuous audit approaches and a move away from base transactional sample testing.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>It's not without challenges though, as the requirements continue to be more onerous, quality continues to be driven up and up and up, and time is finite.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Whilst in the Big Four, my hours were largely 9-5PM, 8PM-3AM and this was the norm most of the year, almost 2 days worth of work per day.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>It was hard, and I was tired.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I finally moved out of there to my current job, still auditing, but the hours were much better.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>The profession continues to move forward, but challenges on why people can rely on auditors continues with scandals not infrequent enough and each one undermines again that it only takes one bad partner signing one bad opinion to undo the trust and good work of thousand before them.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>It's a heavy responsibility to know that you can't be one of the bad ones when actually it is so easy to earn the paycheck and not do the long hours that audit requires.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I doubt this will change any time soon.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Away from this, as a foreigner who came here in 2004 and has made a bit of a life for themselves in the UK, the 2010s really saw quite the change in how I perceived myself and my life in the UK.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>In 2010 I would have happily seen myself staying here for life with wife and kids.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Brexit and the absurd stupidity that was the vote to leave the EU was firmly something that I perceived as being an attempt by the Great British Public to tell me my place really isn't here.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I'm taking one of your jobs.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I've stolen a house for a Brit.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>In my very few trips to the NHS I'm scrounging off the system designed for the Brits, not us foreigners.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>It undermines the very point of why it was so great to move here, undoes a lot of the trust I had in British institutions to be fair and sensible, and frays even family bonds as my wife has family who voted FOR Brexit, which I have taken quite personally.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>Now as COVID hits and both COVID and Brexit are on the calendar for 2020 I am more than ever aware that the long-term future is nowhere near certain here, despite my Settled Status and secure job.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I have always been a nomad, growing up as a Third Culture Kid, but this just adds to that sense of alienation and disappointment in not really being "at home" once more.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>The rest of the decade has been a lovely mix of getting married, having kids, moving house a few times (and always being a renter since there is no way to be a home-owner in London if you had kids when you were in your 20s and didn't get a massive inheritance) and generally living a good life.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Auditing affords good salaries and benefits, which means that I may not be rich enough to own a house, but holidays are easy to go on, trips home to Sweden can be relatively frequent, and life has been good.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>The only other real point of note in the decade was the loss of my mother to Pancreatic cancer, which has profoundly shaped the 4 years since it happened, with sadness every winter as the anniversary of her death looms, and always having certain topics that bring out that slight sadness in me.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It also means I now donate to charity monthly, specifically Pancreatic Cancer research and general cancer charities.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>The grief was incredible.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>2020-2030 The decade ahead is much harder to tell.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Had the question been posed in January I would likely have said much the same but with a newer version of my iPhone.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Coronavirus has destroyed every semblance of normalcy here, with the notion of office work, and of frequent travel, seemingly smashed to pieces.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>The upshot of this is that locality now matters like never before.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Having local groups to support one another, local businesses you can visit and rely on for goods (even if they import from abroad), and finding more creative ways to connect and socialize.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This is likely the new norm.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I can't see commuting being standard at the end of this, which might reduce travel but also reduce stress of commuting and crowded bloody trains.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I also think that it will mean we are now much better equipped for the greatest challenge facing us, climate change.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>This now has shown that there are ways to massively reduce greenhouse gas emissions and pollution, in a way I think before COVID we all thought was "too big a sacrifice" and we felt we couldn't make the changes ourselves to make a sustainable go of it.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>This has shattered that perception.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Flights for business should basically not be a thing anymore, neither should car journeys to client meetings.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>The fact that this has dented the issue and shown a brighter way forward gives me tremendous hope for the future.</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I would like to think the coming decade will see more progress and more tolerance in society, with a significantly large shift away from the far right nationalism we've seen in the US and in some parts of Europe (and indeed in some ways the UK with Brexit) as hopefully the fact that these experiments all seem to be failing gives us a chance to reshape society to improve lives for the worst off.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>Again, COVID has shone an uncomfortable light on inequality and how it affects society, and offers a powerful springboard to try and adjust things societally to improve the lives of those We both have a big spoonful of ricotta and fancy olive oil on top.
