I separate all the items out to correspond with the aisles at the supermarket, to try and make our shop quicker so we can spend as little time as possible in there. This takes quite a while, as my boyfriend likes to cook new dishes.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>Last week I made a big birthday cake for our friend who had her birthday in lockdown. A chocolate Cadbury Crunchie cake, with four layers and toffee buttercream. They only live down the road, so we left it on her doorstep for her and her flatmates to enjoy. They dropped off a couple of slices for us on their walk the next day. It went down pretty well, and I’ve been asked to make a cake for another birthday next week.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>Her favourite dessert is sticky</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>rice with mango, so I’m trying to work out how to make that in cake form – I’ll have to make up a</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>recipe, so I spend a while working out how I might do it so I can add ingredients to our shop.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I’ll have</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>to test it out this week, as I’m feeling the pressure!</EMOTIONS>  <EMPLOYMENT>I was planning on doing some work on my PhD after dinner, but it’s already 10:30pm.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>I’ve struggled</EMOTIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>with it for the last few weeks, as I’ve been either very busy with teaching work or feeling really</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>overwhelmed by trying to work through the pandemic, and think about how my work will be</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>affected by the pandemic.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I was supposed to go on five research trips this summer, which probably</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>won’t happen, and I’m feeling behind.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I worked through the weekend trying to get some extra bits</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>done.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>In the end, I decide to relax for a bit.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I scroll through Twitter and find a funny video of a</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>toddler making a pizza, so I forward it to the Whatsapp group I am in for one of my other jobs,</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>where I work in a museum helping children and families with their learning during their visit. The museum has been shut for several weeks, and I really miss all of my colleagues and working with the public.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>We regularly send funny or light hearted videos to each other on the chat, and it’s nice to</ROUTINE> keep in touch. <ROUTINE>Usually I’m not much of a texter, and often get left behind in group chats – but I’ve</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>spoken to people more during the lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I finally get to bed about half past midnight.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>It’s much later than planned, and I know I’ll feel a bit</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>rubbish tomorrow.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Hopefully my boyfriend will be the one to go in and do the shop this week.</ACTIVITIES> We both prefer when I do it, as I know exactly what I’m looking for and I’m much faster! <ROUTINE>But I’ll be tired</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>and busy with marking, plus I’m not sure what to do about this cough.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We might have to</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>compromise on getting exactly the right ingredients tomorrow!</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>Personal Portrait</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I think of myself as working class and educated, fifty-odd years old, a single man who</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>identifies openly as gay and politically left of centre.</ROUTINE> I live alone in a small town in <ROUTINE>Wales.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I originally hail from the North of England.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I ran away at sixteen to find</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>emancipation in London.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I arrived in London as a gauche young Northern teen during</ROUTINE> the early years of the HIV epidemic. <ROUTINE>I did not know it then but I was to become</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>proactively involved.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This experience was seminal and shaped how I developed as a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>citizen and how I have responded to many of life’s challenges, especially the</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>challenge of a Coronavirus Pandemic.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Last year I retired two years earlier than planned from a long career as an NHS</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>psychotherapist working with children and young people, also as a university</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>lecturer involved in vocational degree programmes for nursing, social work, professions allied to medicine and psychology.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>Public service has exhausted me</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>profoundly, but I remain optimistic and hope to retrain as a Horticulturalist or</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Ecologist, when conditions allow.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Diary Entry: Morning</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>This morning I awoke at seven thirty, feeling slightly disturbed in the aftermath of</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>another lucid Lockdown dream.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>The dream had an atmosphere of ‘Film Noir’, set in</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>dark and satanic landscape leaving me haunted by a feeling of menace, a quality</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>pervading a lot of my dreams at present.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Outside, the sounds of the street, much</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>diminished since Lockdown began, were rousing me to get up.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It was about eight</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>o’clock when I climbed out of bed, the sun was shining brightly through the blind but</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>the air was cold and crisp, a contrast to the warmth we have been enjoying recently.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>I have been sitting out in the sun a lot during Lockdown, in a warm corner of my</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>yard, in the company of an old Bay tree and Radio 4.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>News programmes increasingly</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>vex me, often causing me to shout aloud my disdain to the air.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>I make myself a breakfast of fresh fruit, oats and a cup of turmeric tea; it is now eight thirty.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>As I eat and drink, I reflect on what I will say in this diary.</ROUTINE> Uppermost in my <COVIDNEWS>mind at this time of Pandemic and Lockdown, are co-existing, still unprocessed</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>feelings of loss concerning another national tragedy we call Brexit.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I see and hear</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>similar themes and political tactics being played out by Conservative ministers and</ROUTINE> their media Hound Dogs. <ROUTINE>To my mind, they inflict daily their brand of misanthropic</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>ideology on a confused and anxious public whilst trying to create a semblance that</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>they really care.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>It is a regular charge of close friends, whenever we correspond or</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>speak, that this Conservative Government, and others like it around the world, are</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>trying to kill us all.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>That is certainly a widespread feeling, whether or not it is actually</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>true.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I am sometimes reminded of Pastor Martin Neimoller’s poem, “First they came…”.</ROUTINE> I <ROUTINE>tend to keep radical thoughts to myself as there are few people I trust these days, a</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>sad reflection of the populist times in which we live.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>Such nihilistic feelings started to</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>grow in me during the 1980’s HIV epidemic and the terrible reign of Margaret</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Thatcher.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I watched thousands of my brothers and sisters suffer and die, under the</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>not-so-rainbow-like banner of Section 28 and other callous Conservative policies.</ROUTINE> It <ROUTINE>was a rude awakening to the reality of this ‘Great Britain’ for an idealistic young</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>man, a reality that continues to shape and inform the person I am today.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It is not so</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>strange to me, therefore, to see reports about the huge numbers of deaths in our</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>country as a result of Coronavirus’s arrival on our shores with the incumbent</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Government at the helm.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Even less surprising is the awful reality of what is</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>happening to front-line staff, or the most vulnerable members of this ‘Great British’</ROUTINE> society. <ROUTINE>The dependent, locked away and powerless in care homes, or the neglected,</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>hidden away in the wider community.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This is ‘Great British’ Conservatism: it is cruel,</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>apparently genocidal and may well be the undoing of us all.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Thinking about work during the last ten years, through a policy of ‘austerity’, in</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>which I have practiced as a child psychotherapist and teacher, I wonder at the future</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>of humanity, of civilised society and of basic human rights.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I eschew the wealth that</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>creates such polarisation and inequality in our world, and the endless, multiplying</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>forms of indulgent and environmentally destructive technologies, the Internet of</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Things (God help us all), unsecure electronic communications, the erosion of sacred</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>privacies via Zoom and a plethora of equally unsafe Social Media platforms and</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Apps.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>And like Lemmings we flock willingly toward them all…</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It is now ten o’clock and whilst I have been eating and thinking, outside the house, a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>sudden, loud conversation between two men on the street has penetrated my</ROUTINE> reflections. <EMOTIONS>I am triggered to feelings of anger and outrage.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>They are talking about</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>how people “just need to get back to work” whilst loudly repeating sound bites and</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>populist Conservative slogans.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I want to rush to the door and scream at them to shut</ROUTINE> up, to think about what they are saying, to go away. I despair, it is like a Brexit <COVIDNEWS>narrative but in a new guise of Pandemic.</COVIDNEWS> I will have to move to another room to <ROUTINE>calm down and return to this diary once their machinations have ceased and they</ROUTINE> have moved out of earshot. <COVIDNEWS>Death is a permanent state from which there is no</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>return, do these people not understand?</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Diary entry: Midday</ROUTINE> <FOOD>At one o’clock I am calmer after eating a lunch of egg on toast with a cup of Kenya tea.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>I have also done some household chores, loaded the washing machine, washed</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>the dishes, swept the floor etc.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The sun has disappeared behind grey clouds and the</ROUTINE> air remains chill. <ROUTINE>No sunbathing today.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I glance at headlines in the Guardian and listen to World at One on Radio 4 with Sarah Montague.</DIGITAL> I notice that I am not really <COVIDNEWS>reading or listening to the news, not absorbing the information as well as I did just</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>six weeks ago.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Tuesday has become the day when Coronavirus death rates are officially announced.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Today an article in the Guardian claims that the real death toll is currently over 40,000.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>On the radio they claim the death rate is at 32,000.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It is</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>traumatising to read it and to hear it and then to try and make sense of the</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>comment and punditry that surrounds it.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>This grim reality is becoming too difficult</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>for me to bear, I long to be out in the surrounding hills and countryside to connect</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>with something benign, real and natural.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I want to start shouting at the air again.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Diary Entry: Tea time</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It is now five o’clock.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>This afternoon, instead of focussing on the pandemic, I have been trying to read fiction rather than fact, to meditate and rest my mind.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>There are</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>always a lot of chores to be done, but since leaving work I am less inclined to make</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>myself busy.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I have a perverse sense of my life becoming less structured than the</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>current trend amongst Britons, who, it is reported daily, are sharing endlessly on</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Social Media about using Lockdown as a time to create more structure and thus</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>more opportunity for self-improvement.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Not me.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I am loving the silence of my house</EMOTIONS> and the stillness of my life, the sense of freedom from toil that has become my new normal. <EMOTIONS>I feel a strange sense of guilt but at the same time, gratitude, that I can live</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>this way when so many people’s lives have been upturned or destroyed.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I have</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>volunteered to become a Samaritan, but until Lockdown ends and social distancing</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>becomes well practiced, there will be a lengthy wait to join a volunteer training</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>event with the Samaritans.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>At six thirty I am making Soupe Au Pistou from a recipe in the Guardian that I have become addicted to. I also have a batch of sourdough proving, almost ready to go in the oven. As I prepare my food I take time again to be mindful of everything I have</FOOD> <EMOTIONS>to be grateful for.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>I realise that I have not spoken to a living soul today, but that</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>doesn’t upset me.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Lately there have been weeks when I don’t see or hear from</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>anyone since stopping work.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>I find that I love the solitude in my life and Lockdown</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>creates a permissive sense, allowing me to enjoy this time alone, to be by myself.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Diary Entry: Bedtime</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It is eleven o’clock and I am ready for sleep.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I have not been out of the house today for exercise.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>During Lockdown I have been going out for a bike ride first thing in the morning, until a broken chain and puncture put an end to that.</ACTIVITIES> I am waiting for some <ACTIVITIES>parts that I have ordered to arrive by post so I can make essential repairs to my bike,</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>perhaps they will arrive tomorrow.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>I begrudgingly concede that the Internet has</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>some useful application after all.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>For the past few evenings I have been watching re-runs of an American fantasy drama called Lost, probably another reason why my dreams have been so odd and disturbing.</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>However, tonight’s television viewing was interrupted by me thinking</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>about and re-reading what I have written for this diary.</ACTIVITIES> <EMOTIONS>I have decided not to worry</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>about it, even though some of what I have written could make me appear a little</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>clichéd (Guardian, Radio 4, Sourdough etc.).</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>In life I try not to care too much about</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>what others think, to be true to myself.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I manage this with varying degrees of failure</EMPLOYMENT> and success. <COVIDNEWS>So I after a final reading I submit this account of my day in Lockdown</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>with a willing heart and let go of whatever the outcome may be.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Lord, into thy hands</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I commend my spirit!</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>It's 23:27 on Tuesday, 12th May 2020.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>I would consider today an incredibly uneventful day, but I guess it has its place in the unusual context of life in England during the Covid-19 crisis.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I haven't set my own alarm for 7 weeks now, which seems slightly unsettling.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Luckily H, my girlfriend, is still working each day, so I've joined her routine.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>My moving into her flat has happened three months earlier than planned - a rather convenient probation period, you could say - in order for us to isolate together.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>She's a fantastic person, and it's a pleasure to wake up with her each morning.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I made us our coffees while she showered, then we sat down at our respective laptops.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>For about half an hour I answered questions on a German language learning forum as a favour to my part-time employers.</SCHOOL> <RELATIONS>I translate two videos per week for them and they are good friends of mine, so I'm keen to help out a bit now while I have the time.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>I haven't been able to do my normal full-time job as a freelance assistant director on TV commercials since 17th March, just a few days before the UK entered "lockdown".</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>A significant proportion of my day was spent making a small shelf between a new storage unit and the wall. It is the perfect size to house vinyl sleeves while the vinyls are being played on my turntables. I don't usually end my day reflecting on such mundane things, funnily enough, but thanks to this writing task I now understand the reason I spent so long making sure the shelf fits perfectly and is nicely painted to match the wall: I appreciate that H respects my passion for techno music and is accommodating of all my stuff"
column7: so I want for Music Corner to look as nice possible.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>I had tinned tomatoes and melted cheese on toast for lunch.</FOOD> <FOOD>I had a couple of glasses of white wine in the afternoon
column8: and I cooked a delicious veggie pasta dish with pesto for tea.</FOOD> <EMPLOYMENT>I chatted with various friends on WhatsApp
column9: results of which include: a poker match is now arranged for Thursday evening; Martin rated his housemate's cheesecake 10/10; Jack's music taste was influenced by Joy Division's album Unknown Pleasures; Alexa is disappointed that new union guidelines for returning to work don't include specific mentions of props.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I also despaired about the tragedy of the commons.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Why the fuck are most people unable to see that we will surely benefit in the long run from hugely investing in reducing carbon emissions
column12: maybe most people do see it makes sense but still aren't willing to actually vote for a party that prioritises this long-term interest.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>That's a shame
column13: and I think about it very often.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>At about 8pm I was surprised to be offered to work as a First Assistant Director on a music video on Monday.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>I wasn't expecting for shoots to be up and running again for at least another month
column14: so tomorrow I'll find out more about the plans in place.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>I'll need to evaluate whether it's responsible to run a TV set while Covid-19 is still so prevalent.</COVIDNEWS> <DIGITAL>I watched two episodes of Grand Designs
column15: did a German translation and then started this one-day diary.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>The time is now 00:37 on Wednesday
column16: 13th May.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>It took exactly one hour to write this
column17: which doesn't seem very efficient at all
column18: but I've enjoyed the process.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I'm a 29-year-old male in Salford
column19: and I wish anybody reading this has a lovely day.</ACTIVITIES>"

text: I am 83 years of age and female. <ACTIVITIES>I live in Letchworth Garden City in</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Hertfordshire.</ROUTINE> I am married and this year was hoping to celebrate our Diamond <ROUTINE>Wedding Anniversary (60 Years).</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I have sent out eighty invitations and before</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>the lockdown had received many acceptances including one from Canada.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>Obviously, I’m retired but used to be Head of a Physical education Department</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>in a local school and the Head of Special Educational Needs.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>I taught for over</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>forty years and really made myself retire.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I loved teaching and dealing with</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>young people.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>In fact, I’m still in contact with many of them through the social</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>media.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>8.15am - I woke up and immediately thought, already in to week nine of</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>social distancing!</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>8.25am - straight in to shower</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>9.30am - Ready to start my usual exercise for the day - fifty two laps of the garden which is approximately 2.5k. I’ve been doing this six days a week since week two of self-isolating.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My husband had to self- isolate as he’s in remission from Non- Hodgkin’s</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Lymphoma and me, because I was over seventy.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>9.35am - started my walk and finished it twenty five minutes later.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>10.00am - relaxed in lounge and read the paper, whilst listening to the up to</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>date news on Covid19.</COVIDNEWS> <FOOD>11.00am - Decided to make myself some breakfast – two egg omelette with onions and tomatoes and one slice of dark rye bread as toast. Mug of tea.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>Whilst having my breakfast, I listened to the TV.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I started doing my jigsaw which I’ve been trying to do for the last three weeks! I spent quite a long time doing that and in between, I sat down and thought</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>about all the things that had and were going to be missed due to this virus.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>• My grandson’s 18th birthday</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>• A friend’s wedding – cancelled until next year</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>• An invite to the Victory Club in London for a meal in celebration of 75th</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>VE Day.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>• Watching the celebrations of VE Day at Cineworld</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>• My Granddaughter’s wedding in August</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>• Our Wedding anniversary party in September cancelled until next year</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>• All the football and sport in general that I was missing.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I am a season</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>ticket holder for a Premier League football club and I watch practically all sport on TV or live as I go to athletic matches as well.</ACTIVITIES> <SCHOOL>• My other granddaughter’s graduation from University.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>What a disaster 2020 has been!</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>After spending time on the jigsaw, I caught up on my emails and played a couple of games on my laptop.</DIGITAL> <FOOD>Around 5pm I decide to get our meal ready. During the day, we’d had teas, coffees and small snacks e.g. sultana scone. I prepared a sardine salad with jacket potato and we had sliced peaches and ice cream after with a cup of coffee.</FOOD> <DIGITAL>During the evening we watched various TV programmes and I usually finish the night off by watching Morse reruns and go to bed usually around midnight or after.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>I’m getting rather fed up with not being able to go out to socialise and to meet</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>with my family.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>One daughter does our weekly shop and the other picks up bits</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>that we forget during the week.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>They both have to work anyway and we meet</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>in the garden keeping well away from each other.</ACTIVITIES>

<SCHOOL>I am a 16-year-old female, in year 12 studying for my A Levels, living in the East of</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>England with my parents and younger sibling.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>I woke up at 10am this morning, just in time for my first online lesson. We have been using Google Meets since the lockdown begun and lessons last approximately 1 hour, as they would’ve on a normal day.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>I enjoy the lockdown, as I don’t have to wake up as early each morning and I can continue to participate in lessons from the comfort of my bed.</SCHOOL> <COVIDNEWS>According to Boris Johnson, we have gone past the peak of this virus now and we are slowly returning back to normal, after ~50days in complete lockdown due to COVID-19.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I wonder how long it will take for everything to return to</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>its original state, or even if it will.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>We are living in a time of complete uncertainty,</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>but a personal worry of mine is how it will affect travel of the future.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I’m only 16,</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>and I dream of traveling the world in the future, but I am concerned that perhaps</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>flights will be more expensive, due to the damage this virus has caused to the</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>economy and particularly the travel industry.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Anyways, back to my day.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I had</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>another online lesson, this time it was to practice my speaking skills for A Level</SCHOOL> German. <FOOD>After that I went to make vegan mini pancake cereal (it’s a current TikTok trend, it is essentially just tiny pancakes in a bowl with butter maple syrup – healthy!).</FOOD> <DIGITAL>I watched Netflix for about an hour and a half, before finally trudging</DIGITAL> <SCHOOL>upstairs to get some work done for school.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>Despite the lockdown, we seem to be getting more schoolwork than ever, argh.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>I struggled with it for a while, then video</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>called two friends to help them, and eventually I had to go downstairs for dinner</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>with my family.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>After dinner I went for an evening jog in the nearby fields – following the guidelines, in that we are allowed one form of exercise daily.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Once I returned,</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>my family and I watched TV before coming upstairs for the night.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>I then video called my cousin for half an hour before writing this.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Today seemed to be a more eventful</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>day than many others lately, and that is saying something.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>These few months are</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>peculiar for us all, I suppose, but I firmly believe it’ll be an experience to look back</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>on, if nothing else.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Goodnight</ROUTINE>

<ROUTINE>Biography</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Born in Manchester, Lancashire, UK, 1962.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Anglo-Irish Father, a Bus Conductor, Postal Worker and Long Term Unemployed, Died 1982 aged 51.</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>English Mother, a School Dinner Lady, Medical Secretary and Insurance Clerk, Died 1988 aged 59.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>I Attended Roman Catholic Primary School</SCHOOL> Failed 11 Plus <SCHOOL>No Secondary Education, persistent truant – I was bullied, a lot.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>BA (hons) History xxxxxx University 1999.</SCHOOL> <ACTIVITIES>Youth Activities: Scouts, Army and Air Cadets</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Married 1986 at the age of 23, two children by the age of 25, Amicably Divorced in 2009 at the age</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>of 47.</ROUTINE> Now Single. <ROUTINE>Health: Major concern is Arthritis, leading to bilateral knee replacements in 2011 and bilateral hip</ROUTINE> replacements in 2014. <ACTIVITIES>This has affected mobility and reduced my ability to exercise but not my</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>appetite so, obesity and associated high blood pressure are now an issue to.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>Enlisted into the Royal Army Medical Corps as a Combat Medical Technician in 1979 aged 17. Served in the UK, BAOR Germany, Denmark, Falkland Islands, Northern Ireland, Norway, Kenya, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Kuwait, Cyprus and Bosnia. careless.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Unfortunately, there is a lot of people in this country who are ignorant, and they lack compassion meaning that they are breaking the rules or adapting the rules for vulnerable.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>My grandad who lives in Salford has an underlying health condition and my grandad who lives in the next village has dementia on his own so me and my family are concerned.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I have not seen my grandparents, the ones from Pendlebury in Salford, since around January and so it is very hard not being able to seem them during lockdown and we best out of a bad situation!</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>As for my grandad who had dementia, he lives in the next village and is at a stage in his dementia where he is still able to drive and so he keeps driving to our house at least once a week despite us telling him not to because of Coronavirus but every ten to fifteen minutes he asks why the streets are so empty and he has to observe the 2 metre rule in his local shop.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>I know people will probably think that him coming around means he is breaking the rules and us allowing it is also breaking the rules. However, there is literally nothing we can do to stop him from coming around.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>It is usually my mum who, when he comes to the house, stays downstairs in the kitchen with him (she keeps her distance and I would also like to add how brilliant she is when looking out for my grandad) whilst me, my brother and my dad run upstairs so that we distance ourselves to avoid possibly contracting the virus (he does not have the virus but we cannot it in case he does or in case the member of our family who has done the weekly shop has contracted it).</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>It really is heart-breaking hearing him have a conversation with my mum downstairs, with him repeating himself and keep asking why the streets are empty, and I often have to overcome the urge to go downstairs and just give him a hug.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>If he does come around, it is probably the lowest point of my day as although I can hear him, and I know that he is content in himself I feel an overwhelming sense of sadness that I cannot be with him.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>It is not all sad though, as he has an incredible sense of humour, so he may have dementia, but he makes my mum laugh and content in a time of great despair and anxiety.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>I often think because he is happy living in his own head and because he is generally a jolly and happy man, he is somewhat blessed normal that we were used to, but only time will tell.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Exercise has been a vital component of each day as its important to stay healthy and fit during lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> village for some fresh air and I make sure that I do at least an hours exercise a day whether that be High Intensity Interval Training or just a walk with the dog on the bank of the river near my house.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>Yesterday it was my dad's birthday, so we did our best as a family to celebrate, watching a film and doing a quiz that I had created with questions that related to his date of birth.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Again, it may sound boring, but it is so nice to do something with your family when so many others have lost their loved ones, it makes us all very grateful.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Tomorrow is one of my friends 21st birthday so me and my brother are going to drop some presents off at the end of his drive and wish him a happy birthday from a distance, it is not much but, it will be nice to see him and it will hopefully make his day.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>It is a Tuesday so there is now only four days to go until the virtual quiz I have with my mates on a Saturday night. We have a few beers and have a laugh all from the comforts of our own homes.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>We will hopefully be able to meet up non-virtually very soon.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>The other ridiculous thing I forgot to mention is the stupid idea of a return of the footballing season.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Unfortunately, the FA and the Premier League are blinded by money and their obsession with making a profit means more to them then the health of the managers, coaches, players and staff of those who work and play for the football clubs.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>As a massive football and sports fan, I cannot wait for its return, but it is unacceptable that it is potentially returning so soon when hundreds of people are still dying day to day.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>As much as I love football, life is much more important, and it would serve the Football Association well to remember that themselves.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Surprisingly, the eight weeks have passed rather quickly but how much longer will this last?</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Again, time will tell and I just hope that the wonderful NHS does not become overwhelmed and the virus does not claim more victims.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>The new slogan of the government has changed from 'stay at home, protect the NHS, save lives' to 'stay alert > control the virus > save lives', whatever the hell that means.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>The new slogan does not really give me a great deal of confidence so me and my family will stick to the old slogan, we will continue to stay at home, to protect the NHS and key workers, and hopefully save lives, I just hope the rest of the country do the same for as long as it takes.</COVIDNEWS>

teaching and marking in the midst of the lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>This is a largely frustrating and soulless process dictated by the effectiveness, or otherwise, of the technology.</EMOTIONS> <SCHOOL>Remote learning lacks the professional satisfaction of communicating ideas face-to-face, challenging pupils' assumptions and supporting their progress.</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>The camaraderie of classroom and staffroom are missed too.</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>The weeks wear on and wear down interest in a job I usually love being now reduced to e-mails and conference calls.</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>However, I find organising my very messy folders on the school network strangely satisfying - like I'm investing time in better productivity in the future.</SCHOOL> <EMOTIONS>It tells me that I will get through this.</EMOTIONS> <ACTIVITIES>In the afternoon I undertake the weekly household supermarket shop, the only time any of us leave the house and its immediate surroundings.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My wife and I have established a careful routine involving me taking latex gloves (mainly to deter my normal habit of constantly touching my face), bags kept separate for shopping trips and wiping down every product with dilute bleach on my return.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I go straight into a 20 minute run outdoors and then undress completely into the washing machine and shower when I return.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Exercise has been a vital component of my sanity - a 20 minute run every other day and Joe Wicks on the alternate days for 7 weeks now - and I'm probably physically fitter than I have ever been.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>We are lucky to have a second fridge in a separate room with access from outside to use as a "dirty" space, from which items will be released for eating over the next 3 days before anything left is considered safe enough to move to our kitchen.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>No-one in our family could be considered "high risk" but my wife is very risk averse in general whilst I enjoy the planning, execution and evaluation of the operation.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I'm pleased to have reduced the risk by pushing out this visit to 9 days since the last one but the interval since venturing out only heightens the sense of disconnection, even confusion, when I get in the car.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I tell my wife about the behaviour of some other shoppers - a woman who brought her young teenage daughter to the supermarket and sent her from the end of the aisles to weave in and out of the other shoppers to collect products, neglecting social distancing as she went.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>Another woman walked around with a mobile phone distracted from keeping her distance from others and taking an eternity to narrate the choices to her partner.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>This all helps to justify my wife's caution in the lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>She'd lose her temper at these people if she did the shop, she says.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>She is furloughed from her job, which is dependent on customer contact, so she is unlikely to have work for the foreseeable future.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>The Chancellor's announcement about the extension of the scheme to October is big and welcome news.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Astonished too about the political dimension of a measure announced by a Tory minister and welcomed unreservedly by the TUC.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>We feel that it may be a charter for the unmotivated and think about the different culture in Denmark, which we visited on a family holiday, where people buy into a sense of the collective good and trust each other.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My teenage son and daughter dip in and out of schoolwork through the day and it's impossible to know how much they have to do and when by and whether they are just looking at off-topic YouTube clips.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My youngest son, who is in Year 3, cycles between schoolwork and YouTubers talking about Minecraft.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>He is fired up to make crispy cakes from a clip on Seesaw about imperative verbs so we enjoy time after tea heaping out and heating up golden syrup, butter and drinking chocolate with Rice Krispies.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>The healthy eating aspect of the curriculum has lost out badly.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>We have been watching Periodic Videos on the chemical elements daily but forgot for the first time yesterday, so a catch-up on Calcium before looking at Scandium.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>All three children have been continuing instrumental music lessons remotely but none today.</RELATIONS> <EMOTIONS>The current situation feels like a pivot in my life and a strange hybrid of term time and long summer holiday, busy but with (enforced) time to reflect.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>The rhythms that I miss are the sporting ones, football season ending, cricket season beginning.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>The last ten years have been very "task orientated" - getting things done, getting on with career - but in the next ten years I hope now to be more "people orientated" and focus more on family and the personal development of my pupils rather than ticking boxes and following policies.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I think the post COVID-19 world will be more relaxed than before it though it has reinforced the grip of the smartphone on social interaction.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>I hope that hearing birdsong and the river instead of traffic will change people's attitude to the environment but I expect they will return to the culture of consumption and consumerism all so very quickly.</EMOTIONS>

There has been a lot of cherry blossom in our local area but sadly most of this has fallen now.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>When we arrived home we had lunch - homemade vegetable soup.</FOOD> <EMPLOYMENT>This afternoon at work we had a team meeting with around 15 members of staff, carried out via video call.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>We are having fortnightly meetings for the whole team to get together and discuss workload issues.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>Today we talked a bit about our 'recovery plan' for post lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>We will still need to do a lot of remote working for the foreseeable future which I would prefer, as I am anxious about returning to the office.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>I finished work at 4:30 and did some housework.</EMPLOYMENT> <FOOD>Dinner was leftovers from last night - V had made tapas!</FOOD> <DIGITAL>We often go on city breaks to Spain and it has been on our minds since watching an Ainsley Harriott travel/cookery programme the other day, where he went to Granada and Seville.</DIGITAL> <RELATIONS>So my partner made tapas as a surprise and it was fantastic!</RELATIONS> <FOOD>Potato and onion tortilla, patatas bravas with Mojo sauce, ham croquettes, chickpea stew, alioli mayonnaise, and bread rolls. Everything was made from scratch apart from the croquettes which we had in the freezer. What a treat!</FOOD> <ROUTINE>Anyway, tonight we had the leftovers as she had made big portions.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>At 7:30 we had a zoom chat with V's family - her mum, dad, sister and sister's husband.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>We chatted about this and that but I didn't participate much as by this time my migraine was coming back.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>There was a lot of Coronavirus chat which made me feel more anxious - I would rather talk about other things.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>After the group chat I just went to bed.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>We normally watch TV in the evening but I wasn't feeling great and just wanted to lie down.</EMOTIONS> <DIGITAL>In bed I listened to another podcast on my ipad - this time it was the Scottish comedian Limmy on Richard Herring's Leicester Square Theatre Podcast (RHLSTP).</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>When it finished I looked at Limmy's twitch account - he streams videos of himself playing computer games online, one of which involves driving a truck and delivering items.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>In the podcast he had said some people watch it to help them get to sleep so I thought I would give it a try.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Unfortunately there was about an hour of chat at the start which kept me awake, but when he started playing the game I fell asleep after about 2 minutes so it worked quite well!</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>Today has been a fairly standard day for me in lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>If I hadn't had a migraine I would have maybe spent more time doing something creative as I've been doing more of that recently.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>There was not much to report, but to be honest my day might not have been very different under normal circumstances.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Looking back at the past ten years, I feel things have gone from bad to worse in terms of the way the country is being run, and in fact the world as a whole.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>On a personal level it has been a good 10 years in terms of family, friends and job.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>2020 has been a challenge - aside from the pandemic I have had a couple of bereavements which have hit me hard.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Looking forward to the next 10 years, obviously I am hoping for some normality following the pandemic.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>My dad turns 90 next year so I am worrying about his future health, particularly as I am not allowed to visit him because of the pandemic.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I was hoping to travel this year and next but will put it off until things are safe.</ROUTINE>

<RELATIONS>I’m male, 44 years old, married, no children.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Born and grew up in NW London, I now live in a one bed</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>flat in Limehouse, E14; and I work for one of the big museums in London, but I’m currently on</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>furlough due to the coronavirus lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <EMPLOYMENT>I’m also a part-time history PhD candidate at the</EMPLOYMENT> <SCHOOL>University of xxxxx.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>Woke up this morning at 6:45ish, a bit later than usual.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I normally get up quite early as I like to work 8am-4pm, but one of the plus-points of the lockdown and being furloughed is not needing to set an alarm clock or get up super early every morning.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>After a quick wash and eating breakfast (just coffee and toast) whilst looking over social media – mainly to see what friends and family have posted, I wrote a shopping list and got myself ready to head out to the supermarket.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The weather today is</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>bright and sunny again.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>At the moment I generally go food shopping not more than once a week, and it’s often my only trip out.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>I’m managing to live quite frugally at the moment as there’s only me to look after; Most of them seem oblivious to the ‘social distancing’ idea, they brush past far too closely, heavy-breathing as they go.</COVIDNEWS> Most other people on foot seem to keep a <ROUTINE>reasonable distance when passing.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’m not fond of wearing a facemask, although I’m used to doing</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>so when I’m in Japan, where we generally wear them if we have a cold (so as not to spread it), or if</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>the air is particularly dry in winter.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>The main reason I’m not fond of wearing one is that it constantly</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>makes my glasses steam up!</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>– I keep telling myself that it’s also good to wear one at the moment</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>because of hay fever, given all the sunny weather, the strong winds and lack of rain.</ROUTINE> I used to get hay <ROUTINE>fever very badly when I was a child, but it seemed to disappear for most of my life.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Over the last five</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>or so years though it has returned with a vengeance.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>The River Thames was lovely and calm at low tide as I walked to Canary Wharf this morning.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Absolutely no go to the supermarket as it</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>wasn’t busy there either.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I definitely don’t go out at the weekend.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I went out for a stroll on a</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>Saturday at the start of lockdown and decided never again; it was so busy in the fine weather that I took a long detour home via the roads instead as the pavements there were much quieter and emptier than by the River.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Generally most people where I live seem to be staying home.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>In the afternoons I see some of the</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>smaller kids being watched by their parents as they come out to play in the communal courtyard to let off a bit of steam and get some exercise.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I think I’m the only person left on my elsewhere during the lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I’ve not seen them, but I have</ROUTINE> heard them popping back briefly every few weeks or so, presumably just to check their homes are OK and to the Japanese say</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>“shikata ga nai” (“It can’t be helped”), a bit like the French say “c’est la vie.” My wife did joke today</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>that maybe we are lucky, as if we were currently stuck under the same roof perhaps we’d be driving</ROUTINE> each other crazy! <ROUTINE>We are lucky though, as we realise it’s a lot worse for others right now.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>But, like many, we share the</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>worries for the well-being of our elderly relatives, as well as for friends and family who work for the</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>NHS.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>At the moment I only know of two friends who have had the virus and thankfully both</COVIDNEWS> recovered. <ROUTINE>One distant elderly relative passed away due to other health complications weakened by</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>the virus on Sunday.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>I despise the current Government (esp.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>re: the “hostile environment”, that the last few years has witnessed taking place in</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Parliament.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I despair, and can’t help feeling very bleak about the UK’s future.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Hence, I try to avoid</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>the News and people going on about the situation on social media, otherwise it would be an</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>unending tailspin of depression.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>It all seems such a contrast to when I was coming of age, when the</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>Berlin Wall fell and the future seemed so full of hope and promise.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>I can’t quite fathom why the</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>world has so very deliberately thrown all of that away.</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>Had a very light lunch, and then spent the afternoon reading for my PhD (had a bit of a doze too!).</SCHOOL> <SCHOOL>I’ve been hoping to use this lockdown/furlough time to focus on my studies, but it can be hard to focus at times.</SCHOOL> <ROUTINE>And it is odd how the days do seem to whizz sale</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>of the flat is a bit of a worry, along with all the speculation and uncertainty regarding furlough and</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>income heading into the unknown of the long Who knows? – Not having a particularly coherent roadmap from the Government for the current situation as other countries, such as Ireland, seem to have is troubling.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Just having to</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>deal with each day as it comes is all well-and-good, but only up to a certain point.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I hope that this</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>whole period gives people time to pause and really think about how we live and organise ourselves;</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>to try to improve things collectively for a more equitable and just future once the lockdown has</COVIDNEWS> lifted. <EMOTIONS>I hope it’s shown some blinkered people that certain “false idols” aren’t quite so sacred, and</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>consequently some political and economic norms don’t have to remain set in stone.</ROUTINE> We can <ROUTINE>collectively make life better if we choose to; and, especially if, as a society, we think/plan a bit more</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>for the long term, rather than always looking for the “quick wins” and short-term profits.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>It will be</ROUTINE> nice once this is all over. <RELATIONS>I do miss my wife very much, and I would like to get normal life back on</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>track, primarily so that we can get on with building a new life in Tokyo.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>We’ve only been married a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>few years, so we are both very much looking to the future right now.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I cooked dinner around 7pm.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I’m eating well, i.e.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>– healthily, but not quite as much as perhaps I</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>would in normal times, probably because I’m burning a lot less energy, I suppose.</ROUTINE> Some small <ROUTINE>domestic habits have changed for the better given the lifting of usual time pressures; for instance,</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’ve stopped buying tea bags and instant coffee!</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>– Using real tea leaves in a tea pot, and proper</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>ground coffee in a cafetiere is so much nicer, and, happily, feels like far less of a faff!</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>– After dinner I</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>sat down to spend the remainder of this evening writing this diary.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’ve wanted to contribute to</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Mass Observation before, but always been too busy to do so.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This year though seems like an</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>important one in which to take part.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I’ve been thinking a lot about my great grandparents and the</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>“Spanish flu” pandemic of 1918.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>They lived just a few streets away in Poplar, and my grandparents</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>on my Dad’s side were both born that year.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I can’t imagine what it must have felt like to live through that pandemic with a new born baby; <ROUTINE>About me</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I am an 86 year- old woman, married and living in a village in SE England.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Before I</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>retired I worked in various ways in education – I was a lecturer in an FE college,</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>worked for an examination board, then worked as a free-lance trainer, mostly in the voluntary sector.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>I have written text books, I continue to do some research and writing, mostly now concerned with local history.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I have some mobility problems due</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>to arthritis but I can still drive.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Tuesday 12th May 2020</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This morning was sunny – the light shining through the gap in the curtains woke me</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>up at 6am, and the cold wind of yesterday had dropped so promised a better day.</ROUTINE> I <ROUTINE>snoozed until J.</ROUTINE> brought mugs of tea at 7-30am. <ACTIVITIES>I spent the next half an hour sitting up in bed reading and drinking tea. In this lockdown time, amongst other books I find I am re-reading some of the old detective stories I first read years ago.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Today I am reading Dorothy Sayers Nine Taylors – makes more sense now as I know more about change ringing!</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>J.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>got up first, I followed rather slowly, it takes me a long time to get moving easily</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>these days with arthritic ankle and knees.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Over breakfast we were talking about memories from school, and university, having been married now for 60 years we have heard some of these stories before but I never mind that, and there can still be some things that I have not heard before!</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>We lingered over breakfast until 9-15am</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>and J.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>loaded the dishwasher while I went to make the beds.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>This lockdown and self-isolating makes things much slower as we don’t have any reason to get going, we no</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>longer go shopping (daughter does that) or have meetings to go off to.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>After making the beds the next thing I always do is go to my ‘den’ to check my e-mails; today there is nothing to deal with immediately.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Now it is art time!</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Usually on Tuesday I paint with an art group – now not meeting - but I want to carry on making Tuesday ‘Art Day’ so I paint alone.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>Half of my ‘den’ is ‘study’ the other half is ‘studio’,</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>rather grand names for what used to be our son’s bedroom</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>I prepared my paints, using acrylic today, and found a smallish new canvas I must have bought ages ago, I was just about to start painting when F. knocked on the</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>backdoor, she had brought us a shepherd’s pie and some cake; how kind of her to think of us like this.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>After a chat with F, me standing inside the back door and F outside at the appropriate distance, I then returned to continue with the painting. By 10-50am I had covered the canvas with a first coat of paint and it was now time for a break and coffee so I went to put the kettle on!</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>With coffee I ate one of F’s pieces of fudge cake (very good) and read some of The Guardian.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>I find that these</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>days I do a very superficial read through of the coronavirus news , too much of it</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>makes me either depressed or cross.</EMOTIONS> <COVIDNEWS>I have also stopped listening to the news on TV and rarely listen to the radio</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>At 11-30am I returned to painting, to consider what I have done so far and decide what now? One or two more sweeps of the brush and it has to be left to dry so I put it on a shelf at the end of my ‘den’ to be looked at from time to time to decide what may need changing – adding to…etc...</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>It is an abstractish illustration of a Biblical text that I might share with others when we have a Zoom church study group later this week.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>Then I looked at the palate, still lots of paint there. So I find an old painting, done a long time ago, that I don’t want to keep, so, using a palate knife, I use up the paints left in great sweep of colour in swirly forms – fun to do but no great work of art.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Then to clearing up – I used a tear-off paper palate so that just needs tearing off and screwing up to throw away but the brushes need cleaning more carefully. In the end I did quite a lot of clearing out from one of my shelves of art materials – tubes of paint long dried out, newspapers going back to 2016, old sketch pads with a few pages left….what to do with them? Is now the time to have clear-outs?</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Face book messages from friends keep telling me how much clearing out they are doing, but</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>mostly of pantry shelves and food cupboards.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>Lunch at 1-15pm , sandwiches and fruit, then coffee and a further look at The Guardian.</FOOD> <ACTIVITIES>At 2-30pm I went out into the garden and started by taking down a trough on the wall to replant and give a general uplift. First of all I had to open a new bag of compost that we had delivered from the nursery. When the trough was finished I continued to weed, tie up plants, water some and distribute more compost until J appeared to say it was time I stopped, he had made a mug of tea – very welcome..</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>At 5pm we did some washing up and then planned supper.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>We had a lovely avocado that was just perfectly ripe to start with, followed by the Shepherd’s pie F. had brought us and finished by eating the rest of the pudding J had made for last night’s supper – a quick and easy meal to prepare.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>At 5-45pm we settled in the sitting room with some ‘nibbles’ and I had a glass of</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>sherry and J his usual tomato juice, and then into the dining room for the meal.</ROUTINE> We <ROUTINE>have always had a ‘proper’ three course evening meal in our dining room, perhaps</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>rather old fashioned now, but it matters to us.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>After washing up, we have a dishwasher but there are always pans and cutlery to</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>wash by hand, we retire to the sitting room for coffee or in my case tonight</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>peppermint tea.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>For me the rest of the evening was given up to Television. At 7-30pm on BBC4 we both watched The Beauty of Books with some great pictures of the Luttrell Psalter that I had not seen before.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>At 8pm I settled for 2 hours with Life Drawing Live with my sketchbook and pencils which was an appropriate end to my ART DAY.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Finally went to bed at 10-45pm with a book and a mug of cocoa!</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>In many ways during the pandemic I think I am a very lucky person.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>Retired, with a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>dearly loved ‘soul-mate’, a comfortable pension, in a lovely old house we have lived in for over 50 years, a small but pretty garden in a beautiful part of the country.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>A</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>much loved daughter who lives nearby to do shopping, local shops that will deliver,</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>and kind neighbours.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>I can keep in touch with others by phone, e-mail, and now I</DIGITAL> <DIGITAL>have learned a new skill with Zoom.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>For two of the groups I belong to I am editing a</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>newsletter to help to keep in touch – many people are having a much worse time.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>What am I missing?</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Seeing people face to face, being able to go to the shops and</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>choose things myself, to go out for a cup of coffee, or out for a drive, go to church,</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>go to art group (we even had to cancel our exhibition).</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>That is what I shall enjoy</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>when lockdown and self-isolation finally ends - and we might even go on holiday!</COVIDNEWS>

<COVIDNEWS>It might seem strange that an 88 year old man finds the lockdown restrictive, since there seems little else for him to do at that age, but that is certainly not the case for me.</COVIDNEWS> <ACTIVITIES>I suppose that like many pensioners, there has always been plenty to do and I used to spend a lot of my time in some form of fitness activity. On Mondays I would go down to the gym to do an hour under supervision on the equipment and on Thursdays I would go to an organised group session for an hour’s fitness exercise. Wednesday evening my wife and I would go sequence dancing for two and a half hours and another two hours of social sequence dancing on Friday evenings.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>All these activities give rise to social interaction and this has all had to be ended due to the lockdown</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>My wife and I go for a one hour walk most days but we miss being able to see our</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>children and grandchildren, some of whom live locally and others further away.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>One of my joys was to go down to town two or three times a week (about a mile away) walking down to do some shopping and then back on the bus, I do miss not being able to potter around the shops.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I was 13years old when the war started and I grew up in the era of blackout and</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>rationing, but the current state is a completely different experience.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>At least during the</ROUTINE> war there was no enforced separation other than that brought about by the forces, and <RELATIONS>as children we were able to play together in the school playground.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>The big</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>separation was from my father who spent most of the war in the RAF in North Africa,</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Sicily, Malta and Italy and kept in touch through forces mail written on flimsy paper!</RELATIONS> <SCHOOL>I started in University at age 17 in 1949, taking with me my ration book, and stayed 8</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>years for 2 first degrees and a doctorate, going on to work at the Atomic Weapons</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Research Establishment at Aldermaston. From there I went to the Mechanical Engineering Research Laboratory at East Kilbride and thence to xxxxxx University where I spent 30years lecturing and researching in Theoretical Mechanics. After retiring I did some consulting work on underground fracturing and then went on to part time lecturing on Mathematical Modelling at xxxxxx University.</EMPLOYMENT>

<COVIDNEWS>This May 12, 2020, I am 100 and (nearly) one month old and I celebrated my centenary under lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>This made the celebrations rather muted and also a next-door</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>neighbour had just suffered a bereavement.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>I did see six relations and friends including</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>my new great-great-grandson (born a little over a month before lockdown) from a safe</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>distance and one of my friends played the guitar for me and they all sang “Happy</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Birthday”.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>A copy of my card from the Queen (wearing pink) was made and put on the</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>noticeboard for my neighbours to see at the warden-supervised old people’s complex</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>where I have a little bungalow.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>This is set up to help people to live independently in their</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>later years and I moved here with my husband until he died seven years ago.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>I am able to sit by a big window through which I can see people passing as they go to the shops or for their daily exercise.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>It is my window on the world along with my radio on which I listen to BBC Radio 4 several times each day.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>Far fewer people pass my window now than</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>used to and sometimes it can be hardly any.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>About 70% of people walking by are wearing masks.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>The road is normally a very busy thoroughfare and while car numbers</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>are increasing they are nowhere near their old levels.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My degree many, many (too</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>many!) years ago was in bio-chemistry and my scientific and biological interest has given</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>me an understanding of how viruses operate.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I like to look at the electron microscope</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>pictures of the coronavirus with its trumpets sticking out looking to grab onto a thing.</COVIDNEWS> <COVIDNEWS>Perhaps if we have a typical English summer with lots of rain and cold days this might encourage the virus to loosen its grip on us.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I am lucky here in that food is delivered to</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>my home by my daughter who lives ten minutes away.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>I get a wide range of delicious cooking but one of my favourite treats is beef dripping toast and a half-pint of Adnams beer out of a tin from the supermarket.</FOOD> <COVIDNEWS>Before Coronavirus we would go out every weekend to a country pub to have this, but the pubs have closed.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I also enjoy a glass of</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>red wine with my evening meal.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>Last night I had a meat pie from the supermarket and tonight I will have salmon en croute.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>I like a lot of red and green vegetables (for example</ROUTINE> <FOOD>tomatoes and kale) because I know they have a beneficial effect on the body.</FOOD> <ROUTINE>Because I</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>have a heart condition I weigh myself regularly and I know that today I am 6 stone and</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>7lb.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Apart from my daughter and my friendly neighbours my main companion is my 20-</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>year-old Burmese cat, Hatty, who in cat years is even older than me.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My life under</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>coronavirus is very quiet and rather isolated but considering my age it doesn’t make me discontented.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>We went through the war and we had dreadful shortages, but we don’t</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>seem to have those now and although we could go out in those days it wasn’t easy to</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>travel anywhere because there wasn’t any transport.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>People certainly had a team spirit</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>then and we had to support each other because it was dire.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>We were celebrating VE Day last week and I made bunting to hang outside my window where it went alongside</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>the Rainbow “hope” sign and the wardens in the complex gave us all an individual cream tea which I enjoyed in my front room.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>My husband was in D-Day + 1 where he was an</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>artillery officer and landed on the Normandy beaches but when he came home he was</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>able to take up his old life and resume his studies.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>He became a lawyer and a church</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>minister.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMOTIONS>Sitting here, it doesn’t seem so dreadful when you look out, but that’s because</EMOTIONS> we can’t see the viruses jumping up and down on people !!

I’m 42, and I live in central London (Bermondsey), overlooking the Thames, with my two partners, my 8 year old child (co-parented by one of my partners and me), and our dog. <ROUTINE>I’m a writer (of</ROUTINE> <SCHOOL>science fiction and fantasy), and my child is home-educated (all the time not just during the current</SCHOOL> <EMPLOYMENT>situation!), so both my co-parent partner and I work part time, alternate days.</EMPLOYMENT> I’m queer (bisexual) <ROUTINE>and non-binary.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I woke up around 8 — I’ve been trying to sleep a bit more during the lockdown while my time is a</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>bit more flexible — and wrote two stream-of-consciousness pages longhand, a semi-meditative practice I do most mornings.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Then shower, breakfast, reading Slack and Twitter, watering the seedlings in the mini greenhouse.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Today was a work day for me; tomorrow will be a childcare day. I handled some admin — signed and returned a short story contract, submitted another story to an online magazine, provided a cover quote for a friend’s forthcoming novella — then wrote for a couple of hours, working on some new scenes in the novel I’m currently editing.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>I was able to fit in a writing exercise from a book by Ursula Le Guin (“Steering the Craft”) before lunch.</ACTIVITIES> <FOOD>Lunch was bread, hummus, and roast tomato stuff, eaten while I checked in on my friends on</FOOD> <COVIDNEWS>various Slacks and read the Guardian coronavirus live blog and a few articles.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>One of my partners</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>got the contract through for his new job at lunchtime, too, which was a relief as he’s been waiting for the paperwork.</EMPLOYMENT> <DIGITAL>Read Twitter and discovered to my joy that the Hamilton film will be coming out in July. Told my child who was thrilled — we both love the music.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>I worked on the novel and on a novelette/short story project in the afternoon and then walked the dog.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>She is getting long (1.5-2 mile) walks at the moment as I’m only allowed out once a day for exercise so want to make the most of it!</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>A few people were wearing masks; most people were consciously staying clear of one another; a couple of small groups were playing football; a few people were sitting out on the grass.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>It was nice and sunny for it.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>I sat out in the garden after that and wrote some notes for a different short story; talked to my kid</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>about doing his own Mass Observation diary; watered the garden.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>The nasturtiums are trying to escape out onto the path again, and I had to put the kale and tomatoes back in the greenhouse — I’m hardening them off ready to plant out soon.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Spent a while after that hanging out with some friends on Slack, and thinking about the volunteer work I’m doing and whether it’s possible for me to do anything more.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>Wrote a quick email to my MP about putting the government under pressure re coronavirus test/trace policy and how they’re implementing it. (My concern being that at present they are not doing so well enough, fast enough, or indeed ‘enough’ at all.)</COVIDNEWS> <FOOD>Roast potatoes and chickpea stew for dinner at 18:45 (not my turn to cook!), and a conversation</FOOD> <ROUTINE>about the testing issue.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>My kid’s bedtime at 19:30, and I read two chapters from “The Wizards Of Once: Twice Magic” to him (we’re getting close to the end!).</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Wrote a letter to the life prisoner I’ve been penpals with for about a decade now.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Had a bath with lavender oil and read the latest Lois McMaster Bujold novella, “The Physicians of Vilnoc”, and a page of the book I’m very slowly reading in German, “Die unendliche Geschichte” (The Never-ending Story).</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I also wove in the ends of the jumper I’ve been knitting for myself, in preparation for the final stage tomorrow of steeking it (cutting it down the front!) to insert a zip.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Went to bed around 11 and read for 15 minutes (another Bujold novella) before turning the light out.</ACTIVITIES>

Breath of the Wild I am a 28-year-old man living in a privately rented flat in Worthing, West Sussex. <ROUTINE>I share the flat with a flatmate in order to reduce the cost of rent and bills.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I work for a fairly large company that acquires second-hand items and lists them for resale online.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Due to the coronavirus crisis, stock dried up and I was sent home on furlough, which means the government pays the cost of my wages to my employer.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>It's a relief to still have a job, and I can get by comfortably enough on 80% pay.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>At the time of writing, I am on my eighth week of furlough.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>I got up at around 9:30, which is my "new normal", and ate breakfast.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>My father lives about twenty miles away.</RELATIONS> <EMPLOYMENT>Like me, he has been furloughed for some time.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>On Sunday, the Prime Minister announced changes to the physical distancing rules, and my dad phoned up to arrange to visit me for a walk.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>He will drive over on Friday, although he can't enter the flat and we must stay at least two metres apart at all times, which will be hard.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>Life on furlough began as a welcome opportunity to relax, read, and do some proper cleaning, but lethargy set in after about three weeks.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>I often feel becalmed and useless, and I sleep poorly without work to tire me out!</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>I'm determined to return to work better and fitter than I was when I left, so I always take my daily exercise and try to walk a little further each time.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Today, I made it to the Shell Garage in East Worthing and then back through town, a 90-minute walk.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>It was extremely quiet, apart from a few short queues outside the chemists and supermarkets (and Poundland - some things never change).</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>After getting back home and eating lunch, I relaxed in front of the TV for a while.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>The barbers don't look likely to reopen until July at the earliest, and I've been looking increasingly dishevelled.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My flatmate offered to cut my hair for me, and it turned out pretty well.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Better than expected, anyway.</ROUTINE> <EMPLOYMENT>I spent an hour working on a creative writing project.</EMPLOYMENT> <EMPLOYMENT>Furlough has freed up a lot of time for writing and editing.</EMPLOYMENT> <ROUTINE>For passion projects, it's the most productive period I've ever had.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>After dinner, I had a nice Zoom call with a couple of guys from work.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Like me, they have been furloughed.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>They all seem to be coping with life in lockdown.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>They've both worked for the company longer than I have, but they are a little nervous about redundancies further down the line, so my own worries have gone up a notch.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Ultimately, I can do is cross that bridge as and when I come to it.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Rounded off the day with some videogames with the flatmate while we waited for a food delivery from Iceland.</DIGITAL> <ROUTINE>It's very hard to book a delivery slot with any supermarket right now - ours turned up at 11pm, which was the only time available for the whole week.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I have kept a regular diary for almost a decade.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I've always found it a good way to reflect.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>This is the first time I have ever felt certain that I'm witnessing history, which is not much of a consolation!</EMOTIONS>

I am 84, male, married and living in Eastbourne at the moment. <EMPLOYMENT>I am an accredited Counsellor, although I only work about 4 or 5 hours a week.</EMPLOYMENT> <COVIDNEWS>Nearly everything has changed since the Corona Virus shutdown.</COVIDNEWS> <RELATIONS>A few days before, my wife and I moved from our home in London to a house in Eastbourne shared as a resource with other members of the problem.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Otherwise I am healthy.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>We came here from London because we couldn’t isolate there, as one son and his girlfriend live there plus another son and his family some of the time.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>Both my wife and I are busy people normally, with interests, sport, contact with friends and work (my wife, 74, works two mornings a week, one-to-one teaching at primary schools).</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Now all those things are changed.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Sessions with clients are on Skype or by phone.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>We go for a walk every day deliveries.</ROUTINE> <RELATIONS>Kind neighbours always ask if we want anything if they are about to shop.</RELATIONS> <ROUTINE>Today I got up at nine after reading the newspaper, whereas normally I would be up about 7.30.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>My wife normally would already be out to prepare breakfast for school at 8.15.</ROUTINE> <FOOD>We had boiled eggs for breakfast.</FOOD> <DIGITAL>Before breakfast I let her know how my cousin in Australia had left a message: one cousin had died and left some money to be shared among his two daughters so they could lighten the mortgage, though of wife saw larks and we both enjoyed the beautiful scenery.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>The weather was beautiful, and my planned physiotherapy exercises for my back can only be done on these walks.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>Back home I continued reading the secondhand book I had bought — Lark Rise to Candleford.</ACTIVITIES> <ACTIVITIES>I have also been reading Windsor Park.</ACTIVITIES> <RELATIONS>My wife was doing some video messages with her school children to give them work to be getting on with.</RELATIONS> <FOOD>We had a light lunch — homemade bread, both of us having made some more this morning — with tomatoes and cheese.</FOOD> <RELATIONS>Afterwards I rested awhile and started reading again, and spending more time together with my wife, which I also feel more relaxed with.</RELATIONS> <DIGITAL>I watched Goggle Box and Taskmaster.</DIGITAL> <ACTIVITIES>Then to bed, but after teeth cleaning, application of medication and cream for the skin, then to read a Wodehouse book.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>I realise that I try to stay light in the present situation, especially before going to bed.</ROUTINE> <ACTIVITIES>As I read, I dreamt that a big menacing demon came into the bedroom and got hold of me while I was trying to get out, but then it melted and I was choking because I had shouted.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>On the whole I feel good about being in Eastbourne, and were it not for the seriousness of the Corona Virus situation, all would be well.</COVIDNEWS>
