My wife very is creative and is using her computerised sewing machine to embroider Christmas pictures for all adult family females, depicting words like 'Joy' and 'Love' and 'Peace' and 'Hope'.</RELATIONS> <COVIDNEWS>So far (touch wood!), the two of us have survived two months of total lockdown with only one or two minor spats.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>I did not drink anything tonight, following this morning's hangover.</ROUTINE> <DIGITAL>Watched TV. 'Would I Lie to You?' - always good for a laugh and a reminder of normal times.</DIGITAL> <COVIDNEWS>Then 'The Thick of It' - bitterly funny but terrifyingly close, one feels, to the Government and Civil Service chaos that's probably going on right now.</COVIDNEWS> <ROUTINE>9-30 pm.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Bed - more troubled sleep.</ROUTINE> Reflections. <EMOTIONS>The past decade has been domestically happy and mercifully healthy, and materially we have not wanted for much.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>But there have also been worrying signs; I've been increasingly aware of social inequities, of the 'Haves' versus the 'Have-nots'.</EMOTIONS> <EMOTIONS>I've felt almost guilty about the plenitude all around.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Formerly right-of-centre in my politics, I have become increasingly left-of-centre over several years.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>Climate change is terrifying and its denial by clowns like Trump and many others makes it even more so.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>I have had an uneasy feeling that it's all been too good to last, and I fear the divisive nationalism that is infecting not only the US, but many European countries.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>Right- wing zealots like Steve Bannon are pushing extremism everywhere, and the fact he has had meetings with Johnson and Rees-Mogg is deeply concerning.</ROUTINE> <ROUTINE>I also predict that Stephen Miller will cause more isolationist trouble in the US; the bigot wrote Trump's inauguration speech and is still one of Trump's very few remaining advisers.</ROUTINE> <EMOTIONS>For our future here in Edinburgh, I only hope Scotland can remain part of the UK, despite the Scottish National Party's best efforts to separate us.</EMOTIONS> <RELATIONS>My wife and I used to travel extensively abroad, but in recent years have been extremely content to take a large villa in North Berwick for a couple of weeks with our family and friends.</RELATIONS> <RELATIONS>My dearest wish is that our children and grandchildren will be able to enjoy long and happy lives in a cleaner, greener world than the one we are shamefully leaving them.</RELATIONS>

<ROUTINE>This is the day as spent by a woman in the North West.</ROUTINE> She is at the beginning of her ninth decade and is still active in a number of capacities. <RELATIONS>She is married, and has a grown-up daughter two hundred miles away.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>She lives in a large semi-detached house with a garden.</ACTIVITIES> <ROUTINE>The day was chilly, with some bright sunlight and a sprinkling of rain.</ROUTINE> <COVIDNEWS>We continued in lockdown mode.</COVIDNEWS> <FOOD>Had breakfast of toast and coffee at 9.00 a.m.</FOOD> <EMPLOYMENT>and then took part in a virtual Zoom meeting about a dispute with a main contractor for a completed building project.</EMPLOYMENT> <RELATIONS>Made coffee for my husband and began the ironing.</RELATIONS> <ACTIVITIES>Ate a light lunch and read The Times.</ACTIVITIES> <DIGITAL>Did the washing up, completed the ironing, took a telephone call from my daughter, and brought in the wheelie bin.</DIGITAL> <EMPLOYMENT>Wrote a summary minute of the morning's meeting and sent it to the chairman, who approved it for circulation; sent it to the regular committee secretary for her to send out.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Did a small amount of cross stitch (a design based on a Lowry painting), spoke briefly to a colleague by What's App video, and lit the fire in the drawing room for the first time in a fortnight.</ACTIVITIES> <EMPLOYMENT>Had dinner and did the washing up, and drafted the obituary of a former colleague on my laptop.</EMPLOYMENT> <ACTIVITIES>Had more coffee, completed reading The Times, and bedtime neared at 11.30 p.m.</ACTIVITIES> <COVIDNEWS>Reflections on the day and more generally: - What is a shock is how the nation moved into so-called lockdown like sliding down a bannister and hitting the floor. There had been a dawning sense of dread for some weeks, but the way we were suddenly cut off without preparation was unprecedented; - Supplies are a great concern and are likely to worsen as the year proceeds and the links between production and distribution are broken. An early casualty was flour, but many standard services juddered to a sudden stop, including the absence of the person who cleans the house; - The prejudice against those of greater years is one of the most forbidding aspects of the present situation and sits as a heavy weight in my consciousness, forming a backdrop to all I do; - I like the idea of a special Mass Observation day.</COVIDNEWS> <EMOTIONS>As a reader of David Kynaston's volumes, I know how cumulatively valuable these collections are; - I am intensely grateful for the continued production of daily newspapers, written by people in whom one can have confidence.</EMOTIONS> <ROUTINE>They are a lifeline.</ROUTINE>
